questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the dimensions of the expression $\displaystyle \oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$. The symbols carry their usual meanings.
questions.solution:
The expression $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$ represents the line integral of the electric field, which corresponds to electric potential or electromotive force.

Electric field $\vec{E}$ is defined as force per unit charge, $\vec{E} = \dfrac{\vec{F}}{q}$.

The dimensions of force $\vec{F}$ are $[M L T^{-2}]$ and the dimensions of charge $q$ are $[I T]$.

Therefore, the dimensions of $\vec{E}$ are $\dfrac{[M L T^{-2}]}{[I T]} = [M L I^{-1} T^{-3}]$.

The term $d\vec{l}$ represents an infinitesimal length, which has dimensions $[L]$.

The dimensions of $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$ are the product of the dimensions of $\vec{E}$ and $d\vec{l}$.

Dimensions $= [M L I^{-1} T^{-3}] \times [L] = [M L^2 I^{-1} T^{-3}]$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the dimensions of the expression $\dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$. The symbols carry their usual meanings.
questions.solution:
According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced electromotive force (EMF) is given by $e = -\dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$.

Thus, the dimensions of $\dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ are the same as the dimensions of electric potential or EMF.

Electric potential is defined as work done per unit charge, $V = \dfrac{W}{q}$.

The dimensional formula for work is $[W] = ML^2 T^{-2}$.

The dimensional formula for charge is $[q] = IT$.

Therefore, the dimensions of $\dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ are $\dfrac{ML^2 T^{-2}}{IT} = ML^2 I^{-1} T^{-3}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the dimensions of the expression $vBl$. The symbols carry their usual meanings.
questions.solution:
The expression $vBl$ represents the motional electromotive force (emf).

The dimensions of emf are the same as those of electric potential, which is work done per unit charge.

Dimensions of work $W = [M L^2 T^{-2}]$

Dimensions of charge $q = [I T]$

Dimensions of $vBl = \dfrac{[M L^2 T^{-2}]}{[I T]} = [M L^2 T^{-3} I^{-1}]$

Alternatively, using individual dimensions:

$[v] = [L T^{-1}]$

$[B] = \dfrac{[F]}{[q][v]} = \dfrac{[M L T^{-2}]}{[I T][L T^{-1}]} = [M T^{-2} I^{-1}]$

$[l] = [L]$

$[vBl] = [L T^{-1}] [M T^{-2} I^{-1}] [L] = [M L^2 T^{-3} I^{-1}]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The flux of a magnetic field through a closed conducting loop varies with time according to the relation $\Phi = at^2 + bt + c$. Determine the SI units of $a$, $b$, and $c$.
questions.solution:
By the principle of dimensional homogeneity, each term in the equation $\Phi = at^2 + bt + c$ must have the same unit as the magnetic flux $\Phi$.

The SI unit of magnetic flux $\Phi$ is Weber ($\text{Wb}$).

From Faraday's law of induction, $e = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$, the unit of rate of change of flux is Volt ($\text{V}$). Therefore, $1 \text{ Wb} = 1 \text{ V}\cdot\text{s}$.

The unit of $c$ is the unit of $\Phi$, which is $\text{V}\cdot\text{s}$.

The unit of $bt$ is the unit of $\Phi$, so the unit of $b$ is $\dfrac{\text{Wb}}{\text{s}} = \text{V}$.

The unit of $at^2$ is the unit of $\Phi$, so the unit of $a$ is $\dfrac{\text{Wb}}{\text{s}^2} = \dfrac{\text{V}\cdot\text{s}}{\text{s}^2} = \text{V/s}$.

Thus, the units of $a$, $b$, and $c$ are $\text{V/s}$, $\text{V}$, and $\text{V}\cdot\text{s}$ respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The flux of a magnetic field through a closed conducting loop varies with time according to the relation $\Phi = at^2 + bt + c$. Given that the magnitudes of $a$, $b$, and $c$ are $0.20$, $0.40$, and $0.60$ respectively in SI units, compute the induced emf at $t = 2 \text{ s}$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic flux through the loop is given by $\Phi = at^2 + bt + c$.

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the magnitude of the induced emf is given by:

$|e| = \left| \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \right|$

Differentiating the given expression for flux with respect to time $t$:

$\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}(at^2 + bt + c) = 2at + b$

Substituting the given values $a = 0.20$, $b = 0.40$, and $t = 2 \text{ s}$:

$|e| = 2(0.20)(2) + 0.40$

$|e| = 0.80 + 0.40 = 1.2 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting loop of area $2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field. The field varies over four $10 \text{ ms}$ intervals as depicted in the figure. In which intervals is the induced emf not constant? Neglect the behaviour near the ends of the $10 \text{ ms}$ intervals.
options: $0$ to $10 \text{ ms}$ and $10 \text{ ms}$ to $20 \text{ ms}$
$0$ to $10 \text{ ms}$ and $30 \text{ ms}$ to $40 \text{ ms}$
$10 \text{ ms}$ to $20 \text{ ms}$ and $20 \text{ ms}$ to $30 \text{ ms}$
$20 \text{ ms}$ to $30 \text{ ms}$ and $30 \text{ ms}$ to $40 \text{ ms}$
questions.solution:
According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced emf is given by:

$\varepsilon = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -A \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

Since the area $A$ of the loop is constant, the induced emf $\varepsilon$ is directly proportional to the rate of change of the magnetic field, $\dfrac{dB}{dt}$.

The term $\dfrac{dB}{dt}$ represents the slope of the $B-t$ graph.

From the given graph, we can observe the following:
- In the interval $0$ to $10 \text{ ms}$, the graph is a straight line, meaning the slope $\dfrac{dB}{dt}$ is constant. Hence, the induced emf is constant.
- In the interval $10 \text{ ms}$ to $20 \text{ ms}$, the graph is a curve, meaning the slope $\dfrac{dB}{dt}$ is changing. Hence, the induced emf is not constant.
- In the interval $20 \text{ ms}$ to $30 \text{ ms}$, the graph is a curve, meaning the slope $\dfrac{dB}{dt}$ is changing. Hence, the induced emf is not constant.
- In the interval $30 \text{ ms}$ to $40 \text{ ms}$, the graph is a straight line, meaning the slope $\dfrac{dB}{dt}$ is constant. Hence, the induced emf is constant.

Therefore, the induced emf is not constant in the intervals $10 \text{ ms}$ to $20 \text{ ms}$ and $20 \text{ ms}$ to $30 \text{ ms}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic field in a region varies as shown in the figure. Determine the average induced emf in a conducting loop of area $2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ oriented perpendicular to the field, for each of the four $10 \text{ ms}$ intervals depicted.
questions.solution:
The average induced emf in a loop is given by Faraday's law of induction:

$\varepsilon_{avg} = -\dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} = -A \dfrac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}$

where $A = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ is the area of the loop and $\Delta t = 10 \text{ ms} = 10^{-2} \text{ s}$ is the time interval.

For the first interval ($0$ to $10 \text{ ms}$):
$\Delta B_1 = 0.01 - 0 = 0.01 \text{ T}$
$\varepsilon_1 = -(2.0 \times 10^{-3}) \dfrac{0.01}{10^{-2}} = -0.002 \text{ V} = -2.0 \text{ mV}$

For the second interval ($10$ to $20 \text{ ms}$):
$\Delta B_2 = 0.03 - 0.01 = 0.02 \text{ T}$
$\varepsilon_2 = -(2.0 \times 10^{-3}) \dfrac{0.02}{10^{-2}} = -0.004 \text{ V} = -4.0 \text{ mV}$

For the third interval ($20$ to $30 \text{ ms}$):
$\Delta B_3 = 0.01 - 0.03 = -0.02 \text{ T}$
$\varepsilon_3 = -(2.0 \times 10^{-3}) \dfrac{-0.02}{10^{-2}} = 0.004 \text{ V} = 4.0 \text{ mV}$

For the fourth interval ($30$ to $40 \text{ ms}$):
$\Delta B_4 = 0 - 0.01 = -0.01 \text{ T}$
$\varepsilon_4 = -(2.0 \times 10^{-3}) \dfrac{-0.01}{10^{-2}} = 0.002 \text{ V} = 2.0 \text{ mV}$

The induced emfs are $-2.0 \text{ mV}$, $-4.0 \text{ mV}$, $4.0 \text{ mV}$, and $2.0 \text{ mV}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting circular loop with a radius of $5.0 \text{ cm}$ is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field of $0.50 \text{ T}$. It is then removed from the field over a period of $0.50 \text{ s}$. Determine the average emf generated in the loop during this interval.
questions.solution:
The initial magnetic flux linked with the loop is given by:

$\Phi_{i} = B A = B \pi r^{2}$

Substituting the given values:

$\Phi_{i} = 0.50 \times \pi \times (0.05)^{2} = 0.00125 \pi \text{ Wb}$

When the loop is removed from the magnetic field, the final magnetic flux becomes:

$\Phi_{f} = 0$

The magnitude of the average induced emf is:

$e = \left| \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} \right| = \dfrac{\Phi_{i} - \Phi_{f}}{\Delta t}$

$e = \dfrac{0.00125 \pi - 0}{0.50} = 0.0025 \pi \text{ V}$

$e = 0.0025 \times 3.14 \approx 0.00785 \text{ V} \approx 0.0078 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting circular loop with an area of $1 \text{ mm}^2$ is positioned coplanarly with a long, straight wire at a distance of $20 \text{ cm}$ from it. The electric current carried by the straight wire decreases from $10 \text{ A}$ to zero in $0.1 \text{ s}$. Determine the average emf induced in the loop during this $0.1 \text{ s}$ interval.
questions.solution:
Area of the loop, $A = 1 \text{ mm}^2 = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$

Distance from the wire, $r = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$

Initial current, $I_1 = 10 \text{ A}$

Final current, $I_2 = 0 \text{ A}$

Time interval, $\Delta t = 0.1 \text{ s}$

Since the loop is very small, the magnetic field can be considered uniform over its area.

Magnetic field at distance $r$ due to the straight wire is $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}$

Initial magnetic flux through the loop is $\Phi_1 = B A = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 A}{2 \pi r}$

Final magnetic flux through the loop is $\Phi_2 = 0$

Average induced emf is $e = \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{\Phi_1 - \Phi_2}{\Delta t}$

$e = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 A}{2 \pi r \Delta t}$

$e = \dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \pi \times 0.2 \times 0.1}$

$e = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-12}}{0.02} = 1.0 \times 10^{-10} \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square-shaped copper coil has edges of length $50 \text{ cm}$ and comprises $50$ turns. It is positioned perpendicular to a $1.0 \text{ T}$ magnetic field. The coil is removed from the magnetic field in $0.25 \text{ s}$. Determine the magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop during its removal.
questions.solution:
Given:
Number of turns, $N = 50$
Edge length, $l = 50 \text{ cm} = 0.5 \text{ m}$
Area of the coil, $A = l^2 = (0.5)^2 = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$
Initial magnetic field, $B = 1.0 \text{ T}$
Time interval, $\Delta t = 0.25 \text{ s}$

The initial magnetic flux through a single turn is:
$\Phi_i = BA = 1.0 \times 0.25 = 0.25 \text{ Wb}$

Since the coil is removed from the magnetic field, the final magnetic flux is:
$\Phi_f = 0$

The magnitude of the average induced emf is given by Faraday's law of induction:
$|e| = N \left| \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} \right| = N \left| \dfrac{\Phi_f - \Phi_i}{\Delta t} \right|$

Substituting the values:
$|e| = 50 \times \left| \dfrac{0 - 0.25}{0.25} \right| = 50 \times 1 = 50 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square-shaped copper coil has edges of length $50 \text{ cm}$ and comprises $50$ turns. It is positioned perpendicular to a $1.0 \text{ T}$ magnetic field. The coil is removed from the magnetic field in $0.25 \text{ s}$ and then restored to its original place over the subsequent $0.25 \text{ s}$. Determine the magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop during its restoration.
questions.solution:
Initial magnetic flux when the coil is outside the magnetic field, $\Phi_{i} = 0$

Final magnetic flux when the coil is restored to its original position, $\Phi_{f} = NBA$

Here, $N = 50$, $B = 1.0 \text{ T}$, and $A = (0.5 \text{ m})^2 = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$

$\Phi_{f} = 50 \times 1.0 \times 0.25 = 12.5 \text{ Wb}$

The magnitude of the average induced emf is given by Faraday's law of induction:

$|e| = \dfrac{|\Delta \Phi|}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{|\Phi_{f} - \Phi_{i}|}{\Delta t}$

Substituting the values:

$|e| = \dfrac{12.5 - 0}{0.25} = 50 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square-shaped copper coil has edges of length $50 \text{ cm}$ and comprises $50$ turns. It is positioned perpendicular to a $1.0 \text{ T}$ magnetic field. The coil is removed from the magnetic field in $0.25 \text{ s}$ and restored to its original place over the subsequent $0.25 \text{ s}$. Determine the magnitude of the average emf induced in the loop during its entire motion.
questions.solution:
The initial magnetic flux linked with the coil is given by $\Phi_i = NBA \cos(0^{\circ})$.

Given $N = 50$, $B = 1.0 \text{ T}$, and $A = (0.5 \text{ m})^2 = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$:

$\Phi_i = 50 \times 1.0 \times 0.25 = 12.5 \text{ Wb}$

The coil is removed from the magnetic field and then restored to its original position. Therefore, the final magnetic flux linked with the coil is the same as the initial flux:

$\Phi_f = 12.5 \text{ Wb}$

The total change in magnetic flux over the entire motion is:

$\Delta \Phi = \Phi_f - \Phi_i = 12.5 - 12.5 = 0$

By Faraday's law of induction, the average induced emf is given by:

$e_{avg} = -\dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}$

Since the total change in flux $\Delta \Phi$ is zero, the average emf induced in the loop during its entire motion is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square-shaped copper coil with edges of length $50 \text{ cm}$ contains $50$ turns. It is placed perpendicular to a $1.0 \text{ T}$ magnetic field. The coil is removed from the magnetic field in $0.25 \text{ s}$ and restored to its original place in the subsequent $0.25 \text{ s}$. The resistance of the coil is $25 \text{ }\Omega$. Assuming the coil moves with uniform velocity during its removal and restoration, determine the thermal energy developed in the coil during its removal.
questions.solution:
Area of the square coil, $A = a^2 = (0.5)^2 = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$

Initial magnetic flux linked with the coil, $\Phi_i = NBA = 50 \times 1.0 \times 0.25 = 12.5 \text{ Wb}$

Final magnetic flux linked with the coil after removal, $\Phi_f = 0$

Since the coil moves with uniform velocity, the rate of change of flux is constant. The induced EMF is:

$E = \left| \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} \right| = \dfrac{12.5 - 0}{0.25} = 50 \text{ V}$

Thermal energy developed during the removal is:

$H = \dfrac{E^2}{R} \Delta t = \dfrac{(50)^2}{25} \times 0.25 = \dfrac{2500}{25} \times 0.25 = 100 \times 0.25 = 25 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square-shaped copper coil with edges of length $50 \text{ cm}$ contains $50$ turns. It is placed perpendicular to a $1.0 \text{ T}$ magnetic field. The coil is removed from the magnetic field in $0.25 \text{ s}$ and restored to its original place in the subsequent $0.25 \text{ s}$. The resistance of the coil is $25 \text{ }\Omega$. Assuming the coil moves with uniform velocity during its removal and restoration, determine the thermal energy developed in the coil during its restoration.
questions.solution:
Area of the coil, $A = (0.5)^2 = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$

Total change in magnetic flux during restoration, $\Delta \Phi = NBA = 50 \times 1.0 \times 0.25 = 12.5 \text{ Wb}$

Since the coil moves with uniform velocity, the induced emf is constant:

$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{12.5}{0.25} = 50 \text{ V}$

Thermal energy developed during restoration is given by:

$H = \dfrac{\varepsilon^2}{R} \Delta t = \dfrac{(50)^2}{25} \times 0.25 = 100 \times 0.25 = 25 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square-shaped copper coil with edges of length $50 \text{ cm}$ contains $50$ turns. It is placed perpendicular to a $1.0 \text{ T}$ magnetic field. The coil is removed from the magnetic field in $0.25 \text{ s}$ and restored to its original place in the subsequent $0.25 \text{ s}$. The resistance of the coil is $25 \text{ }\Omega$. Assuming the coil moves with uniform velocity during its removal and restoration, determine the thermal energy developed in the coil during its entire motion.
questions.solution:
Area of the coil, $A = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$.

Initial magnetic flux through the coil, $\Phi_i = NBA = 50 \times 1.0 \times 0.25 = 12.5 \text{ Wb}$.

Since the coil moves with uniform velocity, the rate of change of flux is constant. During removal, the induced emf is:

$E_1 = \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{12.5}{0.25} = 50 \text{ V}$

Thermal energy developed during removal is:

$H_1 = \dfrac{E_1^2}{R} \Delta t = \dfrac{(50)^2}{25} \times 0.25 = 100 \times 0.25 = 25 \text{ J}$

Similarly, during restoration, the magnitude of the induced emf remains the same because the change in flux and time taken are identical:

$E_2 = 50 \text{ V}$

Thermal energy developed during restoration is:

$H_2 = \dfrac{E_2^2}{R} \Delta t = \dfrac{(50)^2}{25} \times 0.25 = 25 \text{ J}$

Total thermal energy developed during the entire motion is:

$H = H_1 + H_2 = 25 + 25 = 50 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting loop of area $5.0 \text{ cm}^2$ is placed in a magnetic field which varies sinusoidally with time as $B = B_0 \sin \omega t$ where $B_0 = 0.20 \text{ T}$ and $\omega = 300 \text{ s}^{-1}$. The normal to the coil makes an angle of $60^\circ$ with the field. Determine the maximum emf induced in the coil.
questions.solution:
The magnetic flux linked with the loop is given by $\Phi = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} = B A \cos \theta$.

Substituting the given values, $\Phi = (B_0 \sin \omega t) A \cos 60^\circ$.

The induced emf is $\varepsilon = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -B_0 A \omega \cos 60^\circ \cos \omega t$.

The maximum induced emf is $\varepsilon_{\text{max}} = B_0 A \omega \cos 60^\circ$.

Given $B_0 = 0.20 \text{ T}$, $A = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, $\omega = 300 \text{ s}^{-1}$, and $\theta = 60^\circ$.

$\varepsilon_{\text{max}} = 0.20 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 300 \times \dfrac{1}{2}$

$\varepsilon_{\text{max}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 150 = 0.015 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting loop of area $5.0 \text{ cm}^2$ is placed in a magnetic field which varies sinusoidally with time as $B = B_0 \sin \omega t$ where $B_0 = 0.20 \text{ T}$ and $\omega = 300 \text{ s}^{-1}$. The normal to the coil makes an angle of $60^\circ$ with the field. Determine the emf induced at $t = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{900}\right) \text{ s}$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic flux linked with the conducting loop is given by

$\Phi = B A \cos \theta$

Substituting $B = B_0 \sin \omega t$, we get

$\Phi = B_0 A \cos \theta \sin \omega t$

By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the magnitude of the induced emf is

$|e| = \left| \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \right| = B_0 A \omega \cos \theta \cos \omega t$

Given values are:
$B_0 = 0.20 \text{ T}$
$A = 5.0 \text{ cm}^2 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
$\omega = 300 \text{ s}^{-1}$
$\theta = 60^\circ$
$t = \dfrac{\pi}{900} \text{ s}$

Calculating the phase angle $\omega t$:

$\omega t = 300 \times \dfrac{\pi}{900} = \dfrac{\pi}{3} \text{ rad} = 60^\circ$

Substituting the values into the emf equation:

$|e| = (0.20) \times (5.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times (300) \times \cos 60^\circ \times \cos 60^\circ$

$|e| = 0.20 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 300 \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$

$|e| = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 300 \times 0.25$

$|e| = 75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting loop of area $5.0 \text{ cm}^2$ is placed in a magnetic field which varies sinusoidally with time as $B = B_0 \sin \omega t$ where $B_0 = 0.20 \text{ T}$ and $\omega = 300 \text{ s}^{-1}$. The normal to the coil makes an angle of $60^\circ$ with the field. Determine the emf induced at $t = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{600}\right) \text{ s}$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic flux through the loop is given by $\Phi = B A \cos \theta$

Substituting $B = B_0 \sin \omega t$, we get:

$\Phi = B_0 A \cos \theta \sin \omega t$

According to Faraday's law, the induced emf is:

$e = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -B_0 A \omega \cos \theta \cos \omega t$

Substituting $\omega = 300 \text{ s}^{-1}$ and $t = \dfrac{\pi}{600} \text{ s}$:

$\omega t = 300 \times \dfrac{\pi}{600} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$

Since $\cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right) = 0$, the induced emf at this instant is zero, regardless of the values of $A$, $B_0$, and $\theta$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As illustrated in the figure, a conducting square loop is positioned parallel to the pole-faces of a ring magnet. Each pole-face has an area of $1\text{ cm}^2$, and the magnetic field between the poles is $0.10 \text{ T}$. All of the wires forming the loop are situated completely outside the magnetic field. Determine the average emf induced in the loop if the magnet is withdrawn in $1.0 \text{ s}$.
questions.solution:
Given:
Magnetic field between the poles, $B = 0.10 \text{ T}$
Area of each pole-face, $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
Time taken to withdraw the magnet, $\Delta t = 1.0 \text{ s}$

Since all the wires of the loop are outside the magnetic field, the magnetic flux passes only through the region between the pole-faces. The plane of the loop is parallel to the pole-faces, so the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop.

The initial magnetic flux linked with the loop is:
$\Phi_i = B \times A = 0.10 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-5} \text{ Wb}$

When the magnet is completely withdrawn, the final magnetic flux is:
$\Phi_f = 0$

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the magnitude of the average induced emf is:
$|e| = \dfrac{|\Delta \Phi|}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{|\Phi_f - \Phi_i|}{\Delta t}$

Substituting the values, we get:
$|e| = \dfrac{10^{-5} - 0}{1.0} = 10^{-5} \text{ V}$

Converting to microvolts ($\mu\text{V}$):
$|e| = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V} = 10 \mu\text{V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting square loop with edges of length $2.0 \text{ cm}$ is rotated by $180^\circ$ around a diagonal in $0.20 \text{ s}$. A magnetic field $B$ is present in the region, being perpendicular to the loop's initial position. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field if the average induced emf during this rotation is $20 \text{ mV}$.
questions.solution:
The initial magnetic flux through the square loop is $\Phi_{i} = BA \cos(0^{\circ}) = BA$.

When the loop is rotated by $180^{\circ}$ around its diagonal, the final magnetic flux becomes $\Phi_{f} = BA \cos(180^{\circ}) = -BA$.

The magnitude of the change in magnetic flux is $|\Delta \Phi| = |\Phi_{f} - \Phi_{i}| = |-2BA| = 2BA$.

The average induced emf is given by Faraday's law of induction:

$\varepsilon = \dfrac{|\Delta \Phi|}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{2BA}{\Delta t}$

Given values are:

$\varepsilon = 20 \text{ mV} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$

Side of the square loop, $a = 2.0 \text{ cm} = 2.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}$

Area of the loop, $A = a^2 = (2.0 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

Time interval, $\Delta t = 0.20 \text{ s}$

Substituting these values into the emf equation:

$20 \times 10^{-3} = \dfrac{2 \times B \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.20}$

$20 \times 10^{-3} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{-4} B}{0.20}$

$20 \times 10^{-3} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} B$

$B = \dfrac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.0 \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting loop with face-area $A$ and resistance $R$ is oriented perpendicular to a magnetic field $B$. The loop is completely removed from the field. Determine the charge that flows through any cross-section of the wire during this process. Note that this quantity depends neither on the shape of the loop nor on the method of withdrawal.
questions.solution:
Initial magnetic flux through the loop is $\Phi_i = BA$.

Final magnetic flux through the loop is $\Phi_f = 0$.

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced emf is given by $\mathcal{E} = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.

The induced current in the loop is $I = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}}{R} = -\dfrac{1}{R}\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.

The charge flowing through any cross-section in time $dt$ is $dq = I dt = -\dfrac{1}{R}d\Phi$.

Integrating both sides, the total charge $Q$ is:

$Q = \int dq = -\dfrac{1}{R} \int_{\Phi_i}^{\Phi_f} d\Phi = \dfrac{\Phi_i - \Phi_f}{R}$

Substituting the values of initial and final flux:

$Q = \dfrac{BA - 0}{R} = \dfrac{BA}{R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long solenoid with a radius of $2 \text{ cm}$ has $100 \text{ turns/cm}$ and carries a $5 \text{ A}$ current. Coaxially positioned inside the solenoid is a coil of radius $1 \text{ cm}$, consisting of $100$ turns and a total resistance of $20 \text{ }\Omega$. This coil is connected to a galvanometer. Determine the charge flown through the galvanometer if the direction of the current in the solenoid is reversed.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field inside the long solenoid is given by $B = \mu_0 n I$

Substituting the given values, $n = 100 \text{ turns/cm} = 10^4 \text{ turns/m}$ and $I = 5 \text{ A}$:

$B = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^4 \times 5 = 2\pi \times 10^{-2} \text{ T}$

The initial magnetic flux linked with the inner coil is $\Phi_i = N B A$

Substituting $N = 100$ and $A = \pi r^2 = \pi (10^{-2})^2 = \pi \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$:

$\Phi_i = 100 \times (2\pi \times 10^{-2}) \times (\pi \times 10^{-4}) = 2\pi^2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Wb}$

When the current in the solenoid is reversed, the final magnetic flux becomes $\Phi_f = -2\pi^2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Wb}$

The magnitude of the change in flux is $|\Delta \Phi| = |\Phi_f - \Phi_i| = 4\pi^2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Wb}$

The charge flown through the galvanometer is given by $q = \dfrac{|\Delta \Phi|}{R}$

$q = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \times 10^{-4}}{20}$

Taking $\pi^2 \approx 10$, we get:

$q = \dfrac{40 \times 10^{-4}}{20} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a metallic square frame of edge $a$ is placed in a vertical plane. A uniform magnetic field $B$ exists in the space, acting perpendicular to the plane of the figure. Two boys pull the opposite corners of the square to deform it into a rhombus. They start pulling the corners at $t = 0$ and move them at a uniform speed $u$. Determine the induced emf in the frame at the instant when the angles at these corners reduce to $60^\circ$.
questions.solution:
Let the angle at the pulled corners be $\phi$.

The length of the diagonal connecting these two corners is given by $x = 2a \cos\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$.

Since each corner is pulled at a uniform speed $u$ in opposite directions, the rate of change of the diagonal length is $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = 2u$.

Differentiating $x$ with respect to time $t$, we get:

$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = -a \sin\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$

$2u = -a \sin\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) \dfrac{d\phi}{dt} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = -\dfrac{2u}{a \sin(\phi/2)}$

The area of the rhombus is $A = a^2 \sin\phi$.

The rate of change of the area is:

$\dfrac{dA}{dt} = a^2 \cos\phi \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$

Substituting the expression for $\dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$:

$\dfrac{dA}{dt} = a^2 \cos\phi \left( -\dfrac{2u}{a \sin(\phi/2)} \right) = -2au \dfrac{\cos\phi}{\sin(\phi/2)}$

We need to find the induced emf when the angle $\phi$ reduces to $60^\circ$. Substituting $\phi = 60^\circ$:

$\dfrac{dA}{dt} = -2au \dfrac{\cos 60^\circ}{\sin 30^\circ} = -2au \dfrac{1/2}{1/2} = -2au$

According to Faraday's law of induction, the magnitude of the induced emf is:

$E = \left| -B \dfrac{dA}{dt} \right| = B |-2au| = 2Bau$

Answer: $2Bau$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metallic square frame of edge $a$ is located in a vertical plane. A uniform magnetic field $B$ acts in the space in a direction perpendicular to the plane of the frame. Two boys pull the opposite corners of the square to deform it into a rhombus. They start pulling the corners at $t = 0$ and displace them at a uniform speed $u$. Determine the total charge which flows through a side of the frame by the time the square is deformed into a straight line, given that the total resistance of the frame is $R$.
questions.solution:
The initial area of the square frame of edge $a$ is:
$A_i = a^2$

The initial magnetic flux linked with the frame is:
$\Phi_i = B A_i = B a^2$

When the opposite corners are pulled and the frame is deformed into a straight line, the area enclosed by the loop becomes zero. Thus, the final area is:
$A_f = 0$

The final magnetic flux linked with the frame is:
$\Phi_f = B A_f = 0$

The magnitude of the change in magnetic flux is:
$\Delta \Phi = \Phi_i - \Phi_f = B a^2$

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the magnitude of the induced emf is:
$E = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$

The induced current $I$ in the frame of total resistance $R$ is:
$I = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{1}{R} \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$

The total charge $Q$ that flows through any cross-section of the frame (and hence through any side) in time $dt$ is $dQ = I dt$. Integrating this gives the total charge:
$Q = \int I dt = \int \dfrac{1}{R} d\Phi = \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{R}$

Substituting the value of $\Delta \Phi$, we get:
$Q = \dfrac{B a^2}{R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metallic square frame of edge $a$ is situated in a vertical plane. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the space, directed perpendicular to the plane of the frame. Two boys pull the opposite corners of the square to deform it into a rhombus. They start pulling the corners at $t = 0$ and displace them at a uniform speed $u$. If the total resistance of the frame is $R$, find the induced current in the frame at the instant when the angles at these corners decrease to $60^\circ$.
questions.solution:
Let $2\theta$ be the angle at the pulled corners. The diagonals of the rhombus have lengths $2x$ and $2y$, where $x = a \cos\theta$ and $y = a \sin\theta$.

The distance from the center to each pulled corner is $x$. Since the corners are pulled at a uniform speed $u$, we have $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = u$.

Differentiating $x = a \cos\theta$ with respect to time $t$:

$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = -a \sin\theta \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = u \Rightarrow \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\dfrac{u}{a \sin\theta}$

The area of the rhombus is $A = \dfrac{1}{2} (2x)(2y) = 2a^2 \sin\theta \cos\theta = a^2 \sin 2\theta$.

The rate of change of area is:

$\dfrac{dA}{dt} = a^2 (\cos 2\theta) \cdot 2 \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = 2a^2 \cos 2\theta \left( -\dfrac{u}{a \sin\theta} \right) = -2au \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{\sin\theta}$

When the angle at the pulled corners decreases to $60^\circ$, we have $2\theta = 60^\circ$, which gives $\theta = 30^\circ$.

Substituting $\theta = 30^\circ$ into the rate of change of area:

$\dfrac{dA}{dt} = -2au \dfrac{\cos 60^\circ}{\sin 30^\circ} = -2au \dfrac{1/2}{1/2} = -2au$

The magnitude of the induced emf is given by Faraday's law:

$E = \left| - \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \right| = B \left| \dfrac{dA}{dt} \right| = 2Bau$

The induced current in the frame is:

$I = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{2Bau}{R}$

Answer: $\dfrac{2Bau}{R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A magnet's north pole is brought downward along the axis of a horizontal circular coil, as shown in the figure. This causes the flux through the coil to shift from $0.35\text{ weber}$ to $0.85\text{ weber}$ in a time interval of half a second. Determine the average emf induced during this duration, and identify if the induced current is clockwise or anticlockwise when observing the coil from the side of the magnet.
questions.solution:
According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the magnitude of the average induced emf is given by:

$|e| = \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}$

Given:
Initial flux, $\Phi_1 = 0.35\text{ Wb}$
Final flux, $\Phi_2 = 0.85\text{ Wb}$
Time interval, $\Delta t = 0.5\text{ s}$

Substituting the values:

$|e| = \dfrac{0.85 - 0.35}{0.5} = \dfrac{0.50}{0.5} = 1.0\text{ V}$

According to Lenz's law, the induced current will oppose the cause that produces it. As the north pole of the magnet approaches the coil, the downward magnetic flux through the coil increases. To oppose this increase, the induced current must produce an upward magnetic field. This means the upper face of the coil must act as a north pole to repel the approaching magnet.

Thus, when viewed from the side of the magnet (from above), the induced current must flow in the anticlockwise direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As depicted in the figure, a square loop of side $5 ext{ cm}$ is moved towards the right at a constant speed of $1 ext{ cm/s}$. The front edge enters the $20 ext{ cm}$ wide magnetic field at $t = 0$. Determine the emf induced in the loop at $t = 2 ext{ s}$.
questions.solution:
Given:
Magnetic field, $B = 0.6 \text{ T}$
Side of the square loop, $l = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
Velocity of the loop, $v = 1 \text{ cm/s} = 0.01 \text{ m/s}$

Distance travelled by the front edge of the loop in $t = 2 \text{ s}$ is:
$x = vt = 1 \text{ cm/s} \times 2 \text{ s} = 2 \text{ cm}$

Since $2 \text{ cm}$ is less than the side length of the loop ($5 \text{ cm}$), the loop is partially inside the magnetic field and is still entering it. The magnetic flux through the loop is changing.

The induced emf $\varepsilon$ is given by the motional emf formula:
$\varepsilon = Blv$

Substituting the values:
$\varepsilon = 0.6 \times 0.05 \times 0.01$
$\varepsilon = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square loop of side $5 ext{ cm}$ travels towards the right at a constant speed of $1 ext{ cm/s}$. The front edge of the loop enters a $20 ext{ cm}$ wide uniform magnetic field of $0.6 ext{ T}$ perpendicular to the plane of the loop at $t = 0$. Determine the emf induced in the loop at $t = 10 ext{ s}$.
questions.solution:
Given:
Side of the square loop, $a = 5 \text{ cm}$
Speed of the loop, $v = 1 \text{ cm/s}$
Width of the magnetic field, $w = 20 \text{ cm}$
Time, $t = 10 \text{ s}$

Distance travelled by the front edge of the loop in $10 \text{ s}$ is:
$d_{\text{front}} = v \times t = 1 \text{ cm/s} \times 10 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ cm}$

The position of the rear edge of the loop at this time is:
$d_{\text{rear}} = d_{\text{front}} - a = 10 \text{ cm} - 5 \text{ cm} = 5 \text{ cm}$

Since the magnetic field extends from $0$ to $20 \text{ cm}$, and both the front edge ($10 \text{ cm}$) and the rear edge ($5 \text{ cm}$) are within this region, the entire square loop is completely inside the uniform magnetic field at $t = 10 \text{ s}$.

When the loop is completely inside the uniform magnetic field, the magnetic flux passing through it is constant ($\Phi = B \times A = \text{constant}$).

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced emf is given by the rate of change of magnetic flux:
$\varepsilon = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$

Since the flux is constant, $\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = 0$.

Therefore, the induced emf in the loop at $t = 10 \text{ s}$ is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire-loop restricted to a plane is rotated within that same plane at a certain angular velocity. A uniform magnetic field is present throughout the region. Determine the emf induced in the loop.
options: A value that depends on the shape of the loop
A value that varies sinusoidally with time
questions.solution:
The magnetic flux through the loop is given by $\Phi = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} = BA \cos \theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the magnetic field and the area vector of the loop.

Since the loop is rotated within its own plane, the direction of its area vector remains unchanged. The magnetic field is uniform and constant throughout the region.

Therefore, the angle $\theta$, the magnetic field magnitude $B$, and the area $A$ of the loop all remain constant during the rotation. This means the magnetic flux $\Phi$ through the loop is constant with time.

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced emf is $e = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.

Since the flux is constant, the rate of change of flux is zero. Thus, the induced emf in the loop is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square loop of side $5 ext{ cm}$ is travelling towards the right at a constant speed of $1 ext{ cm/s}$. The front edge of the loop enters a $20 ext{ cm}$ wide uniform magnetic field of $0.6 ext{ T}$ perpendicular to the plane of the loop at $t = 0$. Compute the emf induced in the loop at $t = 22 ext{ s}$.
questions.solution:
Given, side of the square loop, $a = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
Speed of the loop, $v = 1 \text{ cm/s} = 0.01 \text{ m/s}$
Magnetic field, $B = 0.6 \text{ T}$
Width of the magnetic field, $w = 20 \text{ cm}$

Distance travelled by the front edge of the loop in $t = 22 \text{ s}$ is:
$d = v \times t = 1 \times 22 = 22 \text{ cm}$

The magnetic field exists from $x = 0$ to $x = 20 \text{ cm}$.
At $t = 22 \text{ s}$, the front edge is at $x = 22 \text{ cm}$ (outside the magnetic field) and the rear edge is at $x = 22 - 5 = 17 \text{ cm}$ (inside the magnetic field).
Since the loop is partially outside and exiting the magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked with the loop is changing.
The induced emf is given by the motional emf on the rear edge:
$E = B a v$
Substituting the given values:
$E = 0.6 \times 0.05 \times 0.01$
$E = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a uniform magnetic field $B$ is present within a cylindrical region having a radius of $10 \text{ cm}$. A uniform wire with a length of $80 \text{ cm}$ and a resistance of $4.0 \text{ }\Omega$ is shaped into a square frame and positioned such that one of its sides lies along a diameter of the cylindrical region. Determine the current induced in the frame if the magnetic field increases at a constant rate of $0.010 \text{ T/s}$.
questions.solution:
Length of the wire, $l = 80 \text{ cm} = 0.8 \text{ m}$

Side of the square frame, $a = \dfrac{l}{4} = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$

Radius of the cylindrical magnetic region, $r = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$

Since the side of the square frame ($20 \text{ cm}$) is equal to the diameter of the cylindrical region ($2r = 20 \text{ cm}$) and one side lies along the diameter, the portion of the frame inside the magnetic field is exactly a semicircle.

Effective area of the frame linked with the magnetic field, $A = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi r^2$

$A = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi (0.1)^2 = 0.005 \pi \text{ m}^2$

According to Faraday's law of induction, the induced emf is:

$\varepsilon = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = A \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

Given $\dfrac{dB}{dt} = 0.010 \text{ T/s}$, we get:

$\varepsilon = 0.005 \pi \times 0.010 = 5 \pi \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}$

The induced current in the frame is:

$I = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{5 \pi \times 10^{-5}}{4.0}$

$I = 1.25 \pi \times 10^{-5} \text{ A}$

$I \approx 1.25 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} \approx 3.9 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square loop of side $5 ext{ cm}$ is moving towards the right at a uniform speed of $1 ext{ cm/s}$. At $t = 0$, its front edge enters a $20 ext{ cm}$ wide uniform magnetic field of magnitude $0.6 ext{ T}$ perpendicular to the plane of the loop. Calculate the emf induced in the loop at $t = 30 ext{ s}$.
questions.solution:
Given:
Side of the square loop, $a = 5 \text{ cm}$
Speed of the loop, $v = 1 \text{ cm/s}$
Width of the magnetic field, $d = 20 \text{ cm}$

At $t = 0$, the front edge of the loop enters the magnetic field at $x = 0$.
Distance traveled by the front edge in $t = 30 \text{ s}$ is:
$x_{\text{front}} = v \times t = 1 \text{ cm/s} \times 30 \text{ s} = 30 \text{ cm}$

The position of the rear edge of the loop at $t = 30 \text{ s}$ is:
$x_{\text{rear}} = x_{\text{front}} - a = 30 \text{ cm} - 5 \text{ cm} = 25 \text{ cm}$

The magnetic field exists only in the region from $x = 0$ to $x = 20 \text{ cm}$.
Since the rear edge of the loop is at $25 \text{ cm}$, which is beyond the magnetic field region, the entire loop has completely exited the magnetic field at $t = 30 \text{ s}$.

As the loop is completely outside the magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked with it is zero and constant.
According to Faraday's law of induction, the induced emf is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a square loop of side $5 \text{ cm}$ travels towards the right with a constant speed of $1 \text{ cm/s}$. At $t = 0$, its front edge enters a magnetic field of width $20 \text{ cm}$. Determine the total heat produced in the loop over the time interval from $0$ to $30 \text{ s}$, given that the loop's resistance is $4.5 \text{ m}\Omega$.
questions.solution:
The heat is produced in the loop only when the magnetic flux through it changes, which occurs when the loop is entering and exiting the magnetic field.

Given parameters:
Side of the square loop, $a = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
Velocity of the loop, $v = 1 \text{ cm/s} = 0.01 \text{ m/s}$
Magnetic field, $B = 0.6 \text{ T}$
Resistance of the loop, $R = 4.5 \text{ m}\Omega = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \Omega$
Width of the magnetic field, $w = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$

The induced emf $E$ when the loop is entering or exiting the field is given by:
$E = Bav$
$E = 0.6 \times 0.05 \times 0.01 = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$

The power dissipated as heat during these intervals is:
$P = \dfrac{E^2}{R} = \dfrac{(3 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^{-8}}{4.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W}$

Time taken by the loop to completely enter the magnetic field:
$t_1 = \dfrac{a}{v} = \dfrac{5 \text{ cm}}{1 \text{ cm/s}} = 5 \text{ s}$

The loop travels completely inside the magnetic field from $t = 5 \text{ s}$ to $t = 20 \text{ s}$. During this time, the flux is constant, so no heat is produced.

Time taken by the loop to completely exit the magnetic field (starting at $t = 20 \text{ s}$):
$t_2 = \dfrac{a}{v} = \dfrac{5 \text{ cm}}{1 \text{ cm/s}} = 5 \text{ s}$

Total time during which heat is produced:
$t_{\text{total}} = t_1 + t_2 = 5 \text{ s} + 5 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ s}$

Total heat produced in the loop over the time interval from $0$ to $30 \text{ s}$ is:
$H = P \times t_{\text{total}} = (2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W}) \times 10 \text{ s} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.2 \text{ mJ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the cylindrical region shown in the figure, where the magnetic field increases at a constant rate of $20.0 \text{ mT/s}$. Each side of the square loops $abcd$ and $defa$ is $1.00 \text{ cm}$ long and has a resistance of $4.00 \text{ }\Omega$. Calculate the magnitude of the current in the wire $ad$ assuming both switches $S_1$ and $S_2$ are open.
questions.solution:
When both switches $S_1$ and $S_2$ are open, the outer branches $ef$ and $bc$ act as open circuits.

For an induced current to flow, there must be a closed conducting loop. With both $S_1$ and $S_2$ open, the remaining wires ($ed$, $dc$, $ab$, $fa$, and the central wire $ad$) do not form any closed loop.

Since there is no closed path available for the charges to circulate, no induced current can flow in any part of the circuit, regardless of the changing magnetic flux.

Therefore, the current in the wire $ad$ is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic field in the illustrated cylindrical region increases at a constant rate of $20.0 \text{ mT/s}$. Each side of the square loops $abcd$ and $defa$ has a length of $1.00 \text{ cm}$ and a resistance of $4.00 \text{ }\Omega$. What is the current (magnitude and direction) in the wire $ad$ if switch $S_1$ is open while $S_2$ is closed?
questions.solution:
Since switch $S_1$ is open and $S_2$ is closed, no current can flow through the left loop $defa$. The only closed circuit is the right square loop $abcd$.

The area of the square loop $abcd$ is:
$A = (1.00 \text{ cm})^2 = (1.00 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m})^2 = 1.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

The rate of change of the magnetic field is:
$\dfrac{dB}{dt} = 20.0 \text{ mT/s} = 20.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T/s}$

According to Faraday's law of induction, the induced emf $\varepsilon$ in the loop $abcd$ is:
$\varepsilon = A \dfrac{dB}{dt} = (1.00 \times 10^{-4}) \times (20.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$

The loop $abcd$ consists of four sides ($ab$, $bc$, $cd$, $da$), each with a resistance of $4.00 \text{ }\Omega$. The total resistance $R$ of the loop is:
$R = 4 \times 4.00 \text{ }\Omega = 16.0 \text{ }\Omega$

The induced current $I$ in the loop is:
$I = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-6}}{16.0} = 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \text{ A}$

The external magnetic field is directed into the page and is increasing. According to Lenz's law, the induced current will produce a magnetic field directed out of the page to oppose the change in flux. By the right-hand rule, this corresponds to a counter-clockwise current in the loop $abcd$.

A counter-clockwise current in the loop $abcd$ flows along the path $a \rightarrow b \rightarrow c \rightarrow d \rightarrow a$. Therefore, the current in the wire $ad$ flows from $d$ to $a$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As depicted in the figure, the magnetic field in the cylindrical region increases at a constant rate of $20.0 \text{ mT/s}$. Each side of the square loops $abcd$ and $defa$ possesses a length of $1.00 \text{ cm}$ and a resistance of $4.00 \text{ }\Omega$. Determine the magnitude and direction of the current in the wire $ad$ when the switch $S_1$ is closed and $S_2$ is open.
questions.solution:
When switch $S_1$ is closed and $S_2$ is open, the loop $defa$ forms a closed circuit, while the loop $abcd$ remains open. Therefore, induced current will only flow in the loop $defa$.

The area of the square loop $defa$ is:
$A = l^2 = (1.00 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m})^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

The rate of change of the magnetic field is:
$\dfrac{dB}{dt} = 20.0 \text{ mT/s} = 20.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T/s}$

According to Faraday's law of induction, the induced emf $\varepsilon$ in the loop $defa$ is:
$\varepsilon = A \dfrac{dB}{dt} = (10^{-4} \text{ m}^2)(20.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T/s}) = 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$

The loop $defa$ consists of four sides ($de$, $ef$, $fa$, $ad$), each having a resistance of $4.00 \text{ }\Omega$. The total resistance $R$ of the loop is:
$R = 4 \times 4.00 \text{ }\Omega = 16.0 \text{ }\Omega$

The magnitude of the induced current $I$ in the loop is:
$I = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}}{16.0 \text{ }\Omega} = 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \text{ A}$

To find the direction of the current, we use Lenz's law. The external magnetic field is directed into the page and is increasing. The induced current will create a magnetic field pointing out of the page to oppose this increase. By the right-hand grip rule, a magnetic field pointing out of the page corresponds to a counter-clockwise current in the loop $defa$.

Tracing the loop $defa$ in the counter-clockwise direction ($a \rightarrow d \rightarrow e \rightarrow f \rightarrow a$), the current through the wire $ad$ flows upwards, which is from $a$ to $d$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the cylindrical region depicted, the magnetic field increases at a constant rate of $20.0 \text{ mT/s}$. Each side of the square loops $abcd$ and $defa$ has a length of $1.00 \text{ cm}$ and a resistance of $4.00 \text{ }\Omega$. Find the current in the wire $ad$ if both switches $S_1$ and $S_2$ are closed.
questions.solution:
Let us analyze the induced EMF and currents in the two square loops, $defa$ (left loop) and $abcd$ (right loop).

The area of each square loop is:
$A = (1.00 \text{ cm})^2 = 1.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

The rate of change of the magnetic field is:
$\dfrac{dB}{dt} = 20.0 \text{ mT/s} = 20.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T/s}$

According to Faraday's law of induction, the induced EMF in each loop is:
$\varepsilon = A \dfrac{dB}{dt} = (1.00 \times 10^{-4}) \times (20.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.00 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$

Since the magnetic field is directed into the page and is increasing, Lenz's law states that the induced current will oppose this change by creating a magnetic field out of the page. This requires a counter-clockwise current in both loops.

Let's determine the direction of this counter-clockwise current in the common branch $ad$ for each loop:
For the left loop $defa$, a counter-clockwise current flows in the path $a \rightarrow d \rightarrow e \rightarrow f \rightarrow a$. Thus, it contributes a current from $a$ to $d$ in the wire $ad$.
For the right loop $abcd$, a counter-clockwise current flows in the path $a \rightarrow b \rightarrow c \rightarrow d \rightarrow a$. Thus, it contributes a current from $d$ to $a$ in the wire $ad$.

Since both loops are identical in dimensions and resistance, and are placed in the same uniform changing magnetic field, the magnitudes of the induced currents from both loops are exactly equal.

Because these two equal currents flow in opposite directions through the common wire $ad$ (one from $a$ to $d$ and the other from $d$ to $a$), they perfectly cancel each other out.

Therefore, the net current in the wire $ad$ is zero.

Answer: Zero

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular coil of $N$ turns and radius $a$ is connected to a battery of emf $\mathcal{E}$ through a rheostat. The rheostat has a total length $L$ and resistance $R$. The resistance of the coil is $r$. A small circular loop of radius $a'$ and resistance $r'$ is positioned coaxially with the coil. The centre of the loop is located at a distance $x$ from the centre of the coil. Initially, the sliding contact of the rheostat is at the left end, and from then onwards it travels towards the right at a constant speed $v$. Determine the emf induced in the small circular loop at the instant the contact has slid through half the length of the rheostat.
options: $\dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2 \mathcal{E} R v}{2 L (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2} (R/2 + r)^2}$
$\dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2 \mathcal{E} R v}{L (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2} (R/2 + r)^2}$
$\dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2 \mathcal{E} R v}{2 L (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2} (R + r)^2}$
$\dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2 \mathcal{E} R v}{2 L (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2} (R/2 + r)}$
questions.solution:
Let $y$ be the distance moved by the sliding contact from the left end of the rheostat. Since it moves at a constant speed $v$, we have $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = v$.

From the circuit diagram, the right end of the rheostat is connected to the battery. Therefore, the length of the rheostat included in the circuit is $L - y$, and its resistance is $R_{\text{rheo}} = \dfrac{R}{L}(L - y)$.

The total equivalent resistance of the circuit is:
$R_{\text{eq}} = r + R_{\text{rheo}} = r + R - \dfrac{R}{L}y$

The current in the coil is:
$I = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}}{r + R - \dfrac{R}{L}y}$

The rate of change of current with respect to time is:
$\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \dfrac{dI}{dy} \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dt} = -\dfrac{\mathcal{E}}{\left(r + R - \dfrac{R}{L}y\right)^2} \left(-\dfrac{R}{L}\right) v = \dfrac{\mathcal{E} R v}{L \left(r + R - \dfrac{R}{L}y\right)^2}$

When the contact has slid through half the length of the rheostat, $y = \dfrac{L}{2}$. At this instant, the rate of change of current is:
$\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \dfrac{\mathcal{E} R v}{L \left(r + R - \dfrac{R}{2}\right)^2} = \dfrac{\mathcal{E} R v}{L \left(r + \dfrac{R}{2}\right)^2}$

The magnetic field produced by the large coil at the center of the small loop (at a distance $x$ on its axis) is:
$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I a^2}{2(a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$

Since the small loop has a very small radius $a'$, the magnetic field can be considered uniform over its area. The magnetic flux linked with the small loop is:
$\Phi = B \cdot \pi a'^2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I a^2 \pi a'^2}{2(a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$

The magnitude of the induced emf in the small loop is:
$e = \left| \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \right| = \dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2}{2(a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} \dfrac{dI}{dt}$

Substituting the expression for $\dfrac{dI}{dt}$ at $y = \dfrac{L}{2}$:
$e = \dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2 \mathcal{E} R v}{2 L (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2} \left(\dfrac{R}{2} + r\right)^2}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2 \mathcal{E} R v}{2 L (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2} (R/2 + r)^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a circular coil of $N$ turns and radius $a$ is connected to a battery of emf $\mathcal{E}$ through a rheostat. The rheostat has a total length $L$ and resistance $R$. The resistance of the coil is $r$. A small circular loop of radius $a'$ and resistance $r'$ is positioned coaxially with the coil. The centre of the loop is located at a distance $x$ from the centre of the coil. Initially, the sliding contact of the rheostat is at the left end, and from then onwards it travels towards the right at a constant speed $v$. Determine the emf induced in the small circular loop at the instant the contact begins to slide.
options: $\dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2 \mathcal{E} R v}{2 L (a^2 + x^2) (R + r)^2}$
$\dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2 \mathcal{E} R v}{L (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2} (R + r)^2}$
$\dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2 \mathcal{E} R v}{2 L (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2} (R + r)}$
$\dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2 \mathcal{E} R v}{2 L (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2} (R + r)^2}$
questions.solution:
The resistance of the rheostat in the circuit at time $t$ is given by $R_{rh}(t) = \dfrac{R}{L}(L - vt)$, since the sliding contact starts at the left end and moves right, decreasing the length of the resistive wire in the circuit.

The total resistance of the circuit is $R_{eq}(t) = r + R - \dfrac{R v}{L}t$.

The current in the primary coil is $I(t) = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}}{r + R - \dfrac{R v}{L}t}$.

Differentiating the current with respect to time, we get:

$\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \dfrac{\mathcal{E} \left( \dfrac{R v}{L} \right)}{\left( r + R - \dfrac{R v}{L}t \right)^2}$

At $t = 0$, the rate of change of current is:

$\left. \dfrac{dI}{dt} \right|_{t=0} = \dfrac{\mathcal{E} R v}{L (R + r)^2}$

The magnetic field produced by the large coil at the center of the small loop (at a distance $x$ on the axis) is:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I a^2}{2(a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$

The magnetic flux linked with the small loop of radius $a'$ is:

$\Phi = B \cdot \pi a'^2 = \dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N I a^2 a'^2}{2(a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$

According to Faraday's law, the magnitude of the induced emf in the small loop is:

$e = \left| \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \right| = \dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2}{2(a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} \dfrac{dI}{dt}$

Substituting the value of $\dfrac{dI}{dt}$ at $t = 0$, we get:

$e = \dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2 \mathcal{E} R v}{2 L (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2} (R + r)^2}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\pi \mu_0 N a^2 a'^2 \mathcal{E} R v}{2 L (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2} (R + r)^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular coil of radius $2.00 \text{ cm}$ comprises $50$ turns. A uniform magnetic field $B = 0.200 \text{ T}$ is present in the region, directed parallel to the axis of the loop. The coil is then rotated about a diameter by an angle of $60.0^\circ$. The process takes $0.100 \text{ s}$. Determine the average emf induced in the coil.
questions.solution:
The initial magnetic flux through the coil is given by $\Phi_1 = N B A \cos \theta_1$.

Substituting the given values, $N = 50$, $B = 0.200 \text{ T}$, $A = \pi (0.02)^2 \text{ m}^2$, and $\theta_1 = 0^\circ$:

$\Phi_1 = 50 \times 0.200 \times \pi \times (0.02)^2 \times 1 = 0.004 \pi \text{ Wb}$

The final magnetic flux through the coil after rotation by $60^\circ$ is $\Phi_2 = N B A \cos \theta_2$.

$\Phi_2 = 50 \times 0.200 \times \pi \times (0.02)^2 \times \cos 60^\circ = 0.004 \pi \times 0.5 = 0.002 \pi \text{ Wb}$

The magnitude of the average induced emf is given by Faraday's law of induction:

$e = \left| \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} \right| = \left| \dfrac{\Phi_2 - \Phi_1}{\Delta t} \right|$

Substituting $\Delta t = 0.100 \text{ s}$:

$e = \left| \dfrac{0.002 \pi - 0.004 \pi}{0.100} \right| = \dfrac{0.002 \pi}{0.100} = 0.02 \pi \text{ V}$

Using $\pi = 3.14$, we get:

$e = 0.02 \times 3.14 = 0.0628 \text{ V} = 6.28 \times 10^{-2} \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A closed coil with $100$ turns is rotated in a uniform magnetic field $B = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$ about a diameter that is perpendicular to the field. The angular velocity of rotation is $300$ revolutions per minute. The area of the coil is $25 \text{ cm}^2$ and its resistance is $4.0 \text{ }\Omega$. Determine the average emf developed in half a turn from a position where the coil is perpendicular to the magnetic field, the average emf in a full turn, and the net charge displaced during the half turn, respectively.
questions.solution:
Given:
Number of turns, $N = 100$
Magnetic field, $B = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$
Area of the coil, $A = 25 \text{ cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
Resistance, $R = 4.0 \text{ }\Omega$
Angular velocity, $\omega = 300 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{300 \times 2\pi}{60} \text{ rad/s} = 10\pi \text{ rad/s}$

When the coil is perpendicular to the magnetic field, the initial magnetic flux is:
$\Phi_i = NBA \cos(0^{\circ}) = NBA$

After half a turn (rotation by $180^{\circ}$), the final magnetic flux is:
$\Phi_f = NBA \cos(180^{\circ}) = -NBA$

Change in magnetic flux during half a turn:
$\Delta \Phi = |\Phi_f - \Phi_i| = 2NBA$
$\Delta \Phi = 2 \times 100 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 \times 10^{-4} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Wb}$

Time taken for half a turn:
$\Delta t = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega} = \dfrac{\pi}{10\pi} = 0.1 \text{ s}$

Average emf developed in half a turn:
$E_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.1} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$

For a full turn, the initial and final positions are the same, so the net change in magnetic flux is zero. Therefore, the average emf in a full turn is zero.

Net charge displaced during the half turn:
$q = \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{R} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}{4.0} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A closed circular coil (with both ends joined together) of radius $2.00 \text{ cm}$ comprises $50$ turns and possesses a resistance of $4.00 \text{ }\Omega$. A uniform magnetic field $B = 0.200 \text{ T}$ is present in the region, directed parallel to the axis of the loop. The coil is then rotated about a diameter by an angle of $60.0^\circ$ in a time of $0.100 \text{ s}$. Calculate the net charge crossing a cross-section of the wire of the coil.
questions.solution:
The net charge flowing through a circuit is given by $\Delta Q = \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{R}$, where $\Delta \Phi$ is the change in magnetic flux and $R$ is the resistance.

Initial magnetic flux through the coil is $\Phi_i = N B A \cos(0^\circ) = N B (\pi r^2)$.

Final magnetic flux after rotating by $60^\circ$ is $\Phi_f = N B A \cos(60^\circ) = \dfrac{1}{2} N B (\pi r^2)$.

The magnitude of change in flux is:

$\Delta \Phi = \Phi_i - \Phi_f = \dfrac{1}{2} N B \pi r^2$

Substituting the given values:

$\Delta \Phi = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 0.200 \times \pi \times (0.02)^2$

$\Delta \Phi = 5 \times \pi \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 2 \pi \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$

The net charge crossing the cross-section is:

$\Delta Q = \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{R} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 10^{-3}}{4.00} = 0.5 \pi \times 10^{-3} \text{ C}$

$\Delta Q \approx 0.5 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-3} \text{ C} = 1.57 \times 10^{-3} \text{ C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Comprising $1000$ turns, a coil with a radius of $10 \text{ cm}$ and a resistance of $40 \text{ }\Omega$ is positioned such that its plane is vertical and its axis aligns parallel to the magnetic meridian. This coil is connected to a galvanometer and subsequently rotated by an angle of $180^\circ$ about its vertical diameter. Determine the charge that flows through the galvanometer, given that the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field is $B_H = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$.
questions.solution:
Initial magnetic flux linked with the coil is $\Phi_i = NBA \cos(0^\circ) = NBA$

Final magnetic flux linked with the coil after $180^\circ$ rotation is $\Phi_f = NBA \cos(180^\circ) = -NBA$

Magnitude of change in magnetic flux is $|\Delta \Phi| = |\Phi_f - \Phi_i| = 2NBA$

The charge flowing through the circuit is given by $q = \dfrac{|\Delta \Phi|}{R} = \dfrac{2NBA}{R}$

Substituting the given values:
$N = 1000$
$B = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$
$A = \pi r^2 = \pi (0.1)^2 = 0.01\pi \text{ m}^2$
$R = 40 \text{ }\Omega$

$q = \dfrac{2 \times 1000 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.01\pi}{40}$

$q = \dfrac{60 \times 10^{-5}\pi}{40} = 1.5\pi \times 10^{-5} \text{ C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular coil of one turn with a radius of $5.0 \text{ cm}$ is rotated about a diameter at a constant angular speed of $80$ revolutions per minute. A uniform magnetic field $B = 0.010 \text{ T}$ exists in a direction perpendicular to the axis of rotation. Determine the average emf induced in the coil over a long period.
questions.solution:
The magnetic flux linked with the coil at any time $t$ is given by $\Phi = NBA \cos(\omega t)$.

The induced emf in the coil is given by Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction:

$e = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = NBA\omega \sin(\omega t)$

The average induced emf over a long period of time (which includes many complete cycles) is the time average of the induced emf.

$e_{avg} = \langle NBA\omega \sin(\omega t) \rangle = NBA\omega \langle \sin(\omega t) \rangle$

Since the average value of a sinusoidal function over a complete cycle is zero, the average induced emf over a long period is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular coil of one turn with a radius of $5.0 \text{ cm}$ is rotated about a diameter at a constant angular speed of $80$ revolutions per minute. A uniform magnetic field $B = 0.010 \text{ T}$ exists in a direction perpendicular to the axis of rotation. Determine the maximum emf induced.
questions.solution:
The maximum induced emf in a rotating coil is given by the formula:

$\varepsilon_{max} = NAB\omega$

Given parameters are:
Number of turns, $N = 1$
Radius, $r = 5.0 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
Area, $A = \pi r^2 = \pi (0.05)^2 = 0.0025\pi \text{ m}^2$
Magnetic field, $B = 0.010 \text{ T}$
Angular speed, $\omega = 80 \text{ rev/min} = \dfrac{80 \times 2\pi}{60} \text{ rad/s} = \dfrac{8\pi}{3} \text{ rad/s}$

Substituting these values into the formula:

$\varepsilon_{max} = 1 \times (0.0025\pi) \times 0.010 \times \left(\dfrac{8\pi}{3}\right)$

$\varepsilon_{max} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \pi \times \dfrac{8\pi}{3}$

$\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{200\pi^2}{3} \times 10^{-6}$

Using $\pi^2 \approx 9.87$:

$\varepsilon_{max} \approx \dfrac{200 \times 9.87}{3} \times 10^{-6} \approx 6.58 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$

Rounding off, we get $6.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular coil of one turn with a radius of $5.0 \text{ cm}$ is rotated about a diameter at a constant angular speed of $80$ revolutions per minute. A uniform magnetic field $B = 0.010 \text{ T}$ exists in a direction perpendicular to the axis of rotation. Determine the average of the squares of the emf induced over a long period.
questions.solution:
The magnetic flux linked with the coil at any time $t$ is given by $\Phi = N A B \cos(\omega t)$.

The induced emf is $\varepsilon = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = N A B \omega \sin(\omega t)$.

The square of the induced emf is $\varepsilon^2 = (N A B \omega)^2 \sin^2(\omega t)$.

The average value of $\sin^2(\omega t)$ over a long period (or one complete cycle) is $\dfrac{1}{2}$.

Therefore, the average of the squares of the emf is $\langle \varepsilon^2 \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2} (N A B \omega)^2$.

Given values:
$N = 1$
$r = 5.0 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m} \Rightarrow A = \pi r^2 = \pi (0.05)^2 = 2.5 \pi \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$
$B = 0.010 \text{ T}$
$\omega = \dfrac{80 \times 2\pi}{60} = \dfrac{8\pi}{3} \text{ rad/s}$

Substituting these values:
$N A B \omega = 1 \times (2.5 \pi \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.010 \times \left(\dfrac{8\pi}{3}\right) = \dfrac{20\pi^2}{3} \times 10^{-5} = \dfrac{2\pi^2}{3} \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$

Now, calculating the average of the squares of the emf:
$\langle \varepsilon^2 \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{2\pi^2}{3} \times 10^{-4} \right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{4\pi^4}{9} \times 10^{-8} = \dfrac{2\pi^4}{9} \times 10^{-8} \text{ V}^2$

Using the approximation $\pi^4 \approx 97.41$:
$\langle \varepsilon^2 \rangle \approx \dfrac{2 \times 97.41}{9} \times 10^{-8} \approx 21.65 \times 10^{-8} \text{ V}^2 = 2.165 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V}^2$

Rounding to two significant figures, we get $2.2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V}^2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular coil consisting of one turn with a radius of $5.0 \text{ cm}$ rotates about a diameter at a steady angular speed of $80$ revolutions per minute. A uniform magnetic field $B = 0.010 \text{ T}$ is directed perpendicular to the axis of rotation. If the ends of the coil are connected to a $100 \text{ }\Omega$ resistance, determine the heat produced in the circuit during one minute. Assume the resistance of the coil itself is negligible.
questions.solution:
Given:
Number of turns, $N = 1$
Radius of the coil, $r = 5.0 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
Area of the coil, $A = \pi r^2 = \pi (0.05)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \pi \text{ m}^2$
Angular speed, $\omega = 80 \text{ rpm} = 80 \times \dfrac{2\pi}{60} = \dfrac{8\pi}{3} \text{ rad/s}$
Magnetic field, $B = 0.010 \text{ T}$
Resistance, $R = 100 \text{ }\Omega$
Time, $t = 1 \text{ min} = 60 \text{ s}$

The maximum induced emf in the coil is:
$\varepsilon_0 = N A B \omega$
$\varepsilon_0 = 1 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-3} \pi) \times 0.010 \times \left(\dfrac{8\pi}{3}\right)$
$\varepsilon_0 = \dfrac{20 \times 10^{-5} \pi^2}{3} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-4} \pi^2}{3} \text{ V}$

The average power dissipated as heat is given by:
$P = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{rms}^2}{R} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0^2}{2R}$
$P = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 100} \left( \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-4} \pi^2}{3} \right)^2$
$P = \dfrac{1}{200} \times \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-8} \pi^4}{9} = \dfrac{10^{-8} \pi^4}{450} \text{ W}$

The total heat produced in one minute is:
$H = P \times t$
$H = \dfrac{10^{-8} \pi^4}{450} \times 60 = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-8} \pi^4}{15} \text{ J}$

Using $\pi^4 \approx 97.41$:
$H \approx \dfrac{2 \times 97.41 \times 10^{-8}}{15} \approx 12.98 \times 10^{-8} \text{ J} \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting rod of length $20 \text{ cm}$ undergoes pure translation at a uniform velocity of $10 \text{ cm s}^{-1}$ perpendicular to its length. A uniform magnetic field of $0.10 \text{ T}$ is present, directed perpendicular to the plane of motion. Determine the average magnetic force exerted on the free electrons of the rod.
questions.solution:
The magnetic force acting on a moving charge in a magnetic field is given by $F = qvB \sin \theta$.

For a free electron in the conducting rod, the charge is $q = e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$.

The velocity of the rod is $v = 10 \text{ cm s}^{-1} = 0.1 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

The magnetic field is $B = 0.10 \text{ T}$.

Since the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of motion, the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field is $\theta = 90^{\circ}$, giving $\sin 90^{\circ} = 1$.

The average magnetic force on each free electron is:

$F = evB$

$F = (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (0.1) \times (0.10)$

$F = 1.6 \times 10^{-21} \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular wheel of radius $10.0 \text{ cm}$ is shown in the figure. Its upper half, depicted in dark, is made of iron, while the lower half is of wood. An iron rod connects the two junctions. A uniform magnetic field $B$ of magnitude $2.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$ exists in the region above the central line, as suggested by the figure. The wheel is set into pure rolling on a horizontal surface. Determine the average emf induced during this period if it takes $2.00 \text{ seconds}$ for the iron part to move down and the wooden part to move up.
questions.solution:
The iron part of the wheel forms a closed semicircular conducting loop of area $A = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi r^2$.

Initially, this loop is in the upper half where the magnetic field $B$ is present. The initial magnetic flux through the loop is:
$\Phi_i = B A = \dfrac{1}{2} B \pi r^2$

After $\Delta t = 2.00 \text{ s}$, the iron part moves to the lower half where the magnetic field is zero. The final magnetic flux through the loop is:
$\Phi_f = 0$

The magnitude of the average induced emf is given by Faraday's law:
$e_{avg} = \left| \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} \right| = \dfrac{\Phi_i - \Phi_f}{\Delta t}$

Substituting $r = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $B = 2.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$, and $\Delta t = 2.00 \text{ s}$:
$e_{avg} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} \times (2.00 \times 10^{-4}) \times \pi \times (0.1)^2}{2.00}$

$e_{avg} = \dfrac{10^{-4} \times \pi \times 0.01}{2.00}$

$e_{avg} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$

$e_{avg} = 1.57 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting rod of length $20 \text{ cm}$ undergoes pure translation at a uniform velocity of $10 \text{ cm s}^{-1}$ perpendicular to its length. A uniform magnetic field of $0.10 \text{ T}$ is present, directed perpendicular to the plane of motion. What electric field inside the rod will cause the electric force on a free electron to balance the magnetic force?
questions.solution:
The magnetic force acting on a free electron in the moving rod is given by $F_{m} = e v B$.

The electric force acting on the electron due to the induced electric field is $F_{e} = e E$.

For the forces to balance, $F_{e} = F_{m}$:

$e E = e v B \Rightarrow E = v B$

Substituting the given values, $v = 10 \text{ cm s}^{-1} = 0.1 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ and $B = 0.10 \text{ T}$:

$E = 0.1 \times 0.10 = 0.01 \text{ Vm}^{-1} = 1.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Vm}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting rod of length $20 \text{ cm}$ undergoes pure translation at a uniform velocity of $10 \text{ cm s}^{-1}$ perpendicular to its length. A uniform magnetic field of $0.10 \text{ T}$ is present, directed perpendicular to the plane of motion. Determine the motional emf induced between the ends of the rod.
questions.solution:
Given:

Length of the rod, $l = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$

Velocity of the rod, $v = 10 \text{ cm s}^{-1} = 0.1 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

Magnetic field, $B = 0.10 \text{ T}$

Since the velocity, length, and magnetic field are mutually perpendicular, the motional emf induced across the ends of the rod is given by:

$\epsilon = Bvl$

Substituting the given values:

$\epsilon = 0.10 \times 0.1 \times 0.2$

$\epsilon = 0.002 \text{ V} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metallic metre stick travels with a velocity of $2 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ in a direction that is perpendicular to both its length and a uniform magnetic field of magnitude $0.2 \text{ T}$. Determine the emf induced between the ends of the stick.
questions.solution:
The length of the metallic metre stick is $l = 1 \text{ m}$.

The velocity of the stick is $v = 2 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

The uniform magnetic field is $B = 0.2 \text{ T}$.

Since the stick moves in a direction perpendicular to both its length and the magnetic field, the induced motional emf is given by:

$\varepsilon = Bvl$

Substituting the given values:

$\varepsilon = 0.2 \times 2 \times 1 = 0.4 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spacecraft of width $10 \text{ m}$ travels through interstellar space at a speed of $3 \times 10^7 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. A magnetic field $B = 3 \times 10^{-10} \text{ T}$ is present in a direction perpendicular to the plane of motion. Assuming the spacecraft acts as a conductor, determine the induced emf across its width.
questions.solution:
Given:

Speed of the spacecraft, $v = 3 \times 10^7 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

Width of the spacecraft, $l = 10 \text{ m}$

Magnetic field, $B = 3 \times 10^{-10} \text{ T}$

The induced emf across the width of the spacecraft is given by the formula for motional emf:

$e = Bvl$

Substituting the given values:

$e = (3 \times 10^{-10}) \times (3 \times 10^7) \times 10$

$e = 9 \times 10^{-2} \text{ V}$

$e = 0.09 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two rails of a railway track, insulated from both each other and the ground, are connected to a millivoltmeter. Determine the reading of the millivoltmeter when a train travels on the track at a speed of $180 \text{ km h}^{-1}$. The vertical component of the earth's magnetic field is $0.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$ and the rails are separated by $1 \text{ m}$.
questions.solution:
The speed of the train is $v = 180 \text{ km h}^{-1} = 180 \times \dfrac{5}{18} \text{ m s}^{-1} = 50 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

The axle of the train acts as a conductor of length $l = 1 \text{ m}$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to the vertical component of the earth's magnetic field $B_v$.

The induced motional emf across the axle is given by:

$E = B_v l v$

Substituting the given values:

$E = (0.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}) \times (1 \text{ m}) \times (50 \text{ m s}^{-1})$

$E = 10 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$

$E = 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 1 \text{ mV}$

The reading of the millivoltmeter is $1 \text{ mV}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A right-angled triangle $abc$, formed from a metallic wire, travels at a uniform speed $v$ in its plane as shown in the figure. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the perpendicular direction. Determine the induced emf in the closed loop $abc$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic flux linked with the closed loop is given by $\Phi = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}$.

Since the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is uniform and the loop moves with a constant velocity $v$ in a plane perpendicular to the magnetic field, the area $\vec{A}$ of the loop and the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ remain constant over time.

Thus, the magnetic flux $\Phi$ through the closed loop is constant.

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced emf in the loop is $\varepsilon = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.

Since the flux is constant, $\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = 0$.

Therefore, the induced emf in the closed loop $abc$ is $0$.

Alternatively, the net motional emf in a closed loop moving in a uniform magnetic field is given by $\oint (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$. Since $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ are uniform, this becomes $(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \oint d\vec{l} = 0$ (as the integral of displacement over a closed loop is zero).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A right-angled triangle $abc$, formed from a metallic wire, travels at a uniform speed $v$ in its plane. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the perpendicular direction. Determine the induced emf in the segment $ac$.
questions.solution:
The motional emf $e$ induced in a straight conducting wire of length $\vec{l}$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is given by:

$e = (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{l}$

From the given figure, the segment $ac$ is horizontal and is parallel to the velocity vector $\vec{v}$.

The cross product $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ is perpendicular to both $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$. Since $\vec{v}$ is parallel to the length vector $\vec{l}_{ac}$ of the segment $ac$, the vector $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{l}_{ac}$.

This gives the dot product:

$(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{l}_{ac} = 0$

Thus, the induced emf in the segment $ac$ is $0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A right-angled triangle $abc$, formed from a metallic wire, travels at a uniform speed $v$ in its plane. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the perpendicular direction. Determine the induced emf in the segment $bc$.
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in a straight conductor is given by $\varepsilon = (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{l}$.

The velocity $\vec{v}$ is directed towards the right and the uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is directed outwards (out of the plane of the paper).

By the right-hand rule, the direction of the cross product $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ is downwards (from $b$ to $c$).

This indicates that the magnetic force pushes positive charges towards the end $c$, making the potential at $c$ higher than at $b$. Thus, the end $c$ acquires a positive polarity.

The magnitude of the induced emf in the segment $bc$ is $E = B v l_{\perp}$, where $l_{\perp}$ is the length of the segment perpendicular to both $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$. For the segment $bc$, its entire length is perpendicular to the velocity $\vec{v}$.

Therefore, the induced emf in segment $bc$ is $vB(bc)$, and it is positive at $c$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A right-angled triangle $abc$, formed from a metallic wire, travels at a uniform speed $v$ in its plane. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the perpendicular direction. Determine the induced emf in the segment $ab$.
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in a conductor is given by $E = \int (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$.

The velocity of the loop is $\vec{v} = v\hat{i}$ and the uniform magnetic field is $\vec{B} = B\hat{k}$ (directed out of the plane).

The magnetic force per unit charge is $\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = (v\hat{i}) \times (B\hat{k}) = -vB\hat{j}$.

This indicates that the induced electric field is directed downwards along the negative y-axis.

For the segment $ab$, the effective length perpendicular to the velocity is its projection on the y-axis, which is the length $bc$.

Thus, the magnitude of the induced emf in the segment $ab$ is $vB(bc)$.

Since the force on positive charges is directed downwards, they accumulate at the lower end $a$. Therefore, the potential at $a$ is higher, making it positive at $a$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A copper wire formed into a semicircle of radius $r$ translates in its plane at a constant velocity $v$. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is directed perpendicular to the wire's plane. Determine the emf induced between the ends of the wire given that the velocity is perpendicular to the diameter connecting the free ends.
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in a conductor moving in a uniform magnetic field depends only on its effective length, which is the straight line distance between its free ends.

For a semicircular wire of radius $r$, the effective length vector $\vec{L}$ is the diameter connecting its ends, so $|\vec{L}| = 2r$.

The induced emf is given by $\varepsilon = (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{L}$.

Since the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is perpendicular to the plane of the wire and the velocity $\vec{v}$ is perpendicular to the diameter, the vectors $\vec{v}$, $\vec{B}$, and $\vec{L}$ are mutually perpendicular.

Thus, the vector $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ is parallel to $\vec{L}$, and the magnitude of the induced emf is $\varepsilon = BvL$.

Substituting $L = 2r$, we get $\varepsilon = Bv(2r) = 2rvB$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A copper wire bent into a semicircle of radius $r$ translates within its plane at a constant velocity $v$. A uniform magnetic field $B$ exists perpendicular to the plane of the wire. Calculate the emf induced between the ends of the wire if the velocity is parallel to the diameter joining the free ends.
questions.solution:
The induced emf in a moving conductor in a uniform magnetic field is given by $\varepsilon = (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{l}$, where $\vec{l}$ is the effective length vector joining the ends of the conductor.

For the given semicircular wire, the effective length vector $\vec{l}$ lies along the diameter, so its magnitude is $2r$.

The velocity $\vec{v}$ of the wire is given to be parallel to the diameter, which means $\vec{v}$ is parallel to $\vec{l}$.

Since $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{l}$ are parallel, the vector $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{l}$.

Thus, the dot product $(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{l} = 0$.

Therefore, the induced emf between the ends of the wire is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
