questions.q: A wire of length $10 \text{ cm}$ translates in a direction that makes an angle of $60^\circ$ with its length. The plane of motion is perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of $1.0 \text{ T}$ present in the region. Determine the emf induced between the ends of the wire if its speed of translation is $20 \text{ cm s}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Given:

Length of the wire, $l = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$

Speed of translation, $v = 20 \text{ cm s}^{-1} = 0.2 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

Magnetic field, $B = 1.0 \text{ T}$

Angle between velocity and length of the wire, $\theta = 60^\circ$

The motional emf induced in a straight conductor moving in a uniform magnetic field is given by the scalar triple product $\varepsilon = |(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{l}|$. Since $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{l}$ are in a plane perpendicular to $\vec{B}$, this simplifies to:

$\varepsilon = B v l \sin \theta$

Substituting the given values:

$\varepsilon = 1.0 \times 0.2 \times 0.1 \times \sin 60^\circ$

$\varepsilon = 0.02 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

$\varepsilon = 0.01 \times 1.732 \approx 0.017 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular copper-ring of radius $r$ travels in its plane with a constant velocity $v$. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the space, directed perpendicular to the plane of the ring. Considering various pairs of diametrically opposite points on the ring, between which pair of points is the emf maximum, and what is the value of this maximum emf?
options: At the ends of the diameter parallel to the velocity, $rvB$
At the ends of the diameter perpendicular to the velocity, $rvB$
At the ends of the diameter perpendicular to the velocity, $2rvB$
At the ends of the diameter parallel to the velocity, $2rvB$
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced between any two points A and B of a conductor moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is given by $\varepsilon = (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{l}$, where $\vec{l}$ is the displacement vector from A to B.

Let the velocity of the ring be $\vec{v} = v \hat{i}$ and the magnetic field be $\vec{B} = B \hat{k}$.

The cross product is $\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = (v \hat{i}) \times (B \hat{k}) = -vB \hat{j}$.

The induced emf is $\varepsilon = -vB \hat{j} \cdot \vec{l}$.

For the magnitude of $\varepsilon$ to be maximum, the displacement vector $\vec{l}$ must be parallel to the y-axis, which means it must be perpendicular to the velocity $\vec{v}$.

For diametrically opposite points, the maximum magnitude of $\vec{l}$ is the diameter of the ring, which is $2r$.

Therefore, the maximum emf is induced across the ends of the diameter perpendicular to the velocity.

The maximum value of the emf is $\varepsilon_{max} = vB(2r) = 2rvB$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular copper-ring of radius $r$ travels in its plane with a constant velocity $v$. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the space, directed perpendicular to the plane of the ring. Considering various pairs of diametrically opposite points on the ring, between which pair of points is the emf minimum, and what is the value of this minimum emf?
options: At the ends of the diameter perpendicular to the velocity, $2rvB$
At the ends of the diameter perpendicular to the velocity, $0$
At the ends of the diameter parallel to the velocity, $0$
At the ends of the diameter parallel to the velocity, $2rvB$
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced between any two points of a conductor moving in a uniform magnetic field is given by $\varepsilon = (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot \vec{l}$, where $\vec{l}$ is the displacement vector between the two points.

Here, $\vec{v}$ is in the plane of the ring and $\vec{B}$ is perpendicular to the plane. Thus, $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ is a vector in the plane of the ring, perpendicular to the velocity $\vec{v}$, with magnitude $vB$.

For diametrically opposite points, the displacement vector $\vec{l}$ has a magnitude of $2r$.

The induced emf is $\varepsilon = vB(2r) \cos \theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ and $\vec{l}$.

For the emf to be minimum, we must have $\varepsilon = 0$, which means $\cos \theta = 0$. This implies $\vec{l}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$.

Since $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{v}$, the diameter vector $\vec{l}$ must be parallel to the velocity $\vec{v}$.

Therefore, the minimum emf is $0$, which occurs at the ends of the diameter parallel to the velocity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a wire slides on two parallel, conducting rails positioned at a separation $l$. A magnetic field $B$ is directed perpendicular to the plane of the rails. What force is needed to keep the wire moving at a constant velocity $v$?
questions.solution:
As the wire moves in the uniform magnetic field, a motional emf is induced across its ends, given by $\varepsilon = Bvl$.

However, the two parallel rails are not connected to each other to form a closed loop, meaning the circuit is open.

Because the circuit is open, the induced current $I$ flowing through the wire is zero.

The magnetic force acting on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field is given by $F_m = IlB$.

Substituting $I = 0$, we get $F_m = 0$.

To keep the wire moving at a constant velocity, the net force must be zero. Therefore, the required external force is $0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a long U-shaped wire of width $l$ is positioned in a perpendicular magnetic field $B$. A wire of length $l$ slides on the U-shaped wire towards the right at a constant velocity $v$. The resistance of all the wires is $r$ per unit length. At $t = 0$, the sliding wire is situated close to the left edge of the U-shaped wire. Determine the force needed to maintain the sliding wire's constant velocity $v$ at time $t$.
questions.solution:
At time $t$, the distance moved by the sliding wire towards the right is $x = vt$.

The conducting loop consists of the left edge of the U-shaped wire, the top and bottom segments of the U-shaped wire up to the sliding wire, and the sliding wire itself. The total length of the wire forming the loop is:

$L = l \text{ (left edge)} + vt \text{ (top segment)} + vt \text{ (bottom segment)} + l \text{ (sliding wire)}$

$L = 2l + 2vt = 2(l + vt)$

Since the resistance of all wires is $r$ per unit length, the total resistance of the loop at time $t$ is:

$R = rL = 2r(l + vt)$

The motional emf induced in the sliding wire due to its movement in the perpendicular magnetic field is:

$\varepsilon = Bvl$

The induced current flowing through the loop is:

$I = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{Bvl}{2r(l + vt)}$

The magnetic force acting on the current-carrying sliding wire opposes its motion and is given by:

$F_m = IlB = \left( \dfrac{Bvl}{2r(l + vt)} \right) lB = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{2r(l + vt)}$

To maintain a constant velocity $v$, the net force on the sliding wire must be zero. Therefore, the external force $F$ required must balance the magnetic force:

$F = F_m = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{2r(l + vt)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long U-shaped wire of width $l$ is positioned in a perpendicular magnetic field $B$. A wire of length $l$ slides on the U-shaped wire towards the right at a constant velocity $v$. The resistance of all the wires is $r$ per unit length. At $t = 0$, the sliding wire is situated close to the left edge of the U-shaped wire. If the force required just after $t = 0$ to maintain this motion is $F_0$, find the time at which the required force becomes $F_0/2$.
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in the sliding wire is given by:

$E = Bvl$

Let the position of the sliding wire at time $t$ be $x = vt$. The conducting loop is formed by the left edge of the U-shaped wire (length $l$), the top and bottom horizontal segments (each of length $vt$), and the sliding wire (length $l$).

The total length of the conducting loop at time $t$ is:

$L(t) = l + vt + vt + l = 2l + 2vt$

Since the resistance of all wires is $r$ per unit length, the total resistance of the loop at time $t$ is:

$R(t) = r(2l + 2vt) = 2r(l + vt)$

The induced current in the loop is:

$I(t) = \dfrac{E}{R(t)} = \dfrac{Bvl}{2r(l + vt)}$

To maintain a constant velocity, the external force applied must balance the magnetic force on the sliding wire. Thus, the required force is:

$F(t) = I(t)lB = \dfrac{B^2l^2v}{2r(l + vt)}$

At $t = 0$, the initial force $F_0$ is:

$F_0 = \dfrac{B^2l^2v}{2rl}$

We need to find the time $t$ when the required force becomes $F_0/2$:

$\dfrac{B^2l^2v}{2r(l + vt)} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{B^2l^2v}{2rl} \right)$

$\Rightarrow 2r(l + vt) = 2(2rl)$

$\Rightarrow l + vt = 2l$

$\Rightarrow vt = l$

$\Rightarrow t = \dfrac{l}{v}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a long U-shaped wire of width $l$ is situated in a perpendicular magnetic field $B$. A wire of length $l$ is slid along the U-shaped wire towards the right at a constant velocity $v$. All wires possess a resistance of $r$ per unit length. At $t = 0$, the sliding wire is located near the left edge of the U-shaped wire. Determine the current flowing in the circuit at time $t$.
questions.solution:
The motional EMF induced in the sliding wire moving with velocity $v$ in a perpendicular magnetic field $B$ is given by:

$E = Blv$

At time $t$, the distance moved by the sliding wire is $x = vt$. The circuit forms a rectangular loop. The total length of the wire forming this loop consists of the left edge of the U-shaped wire (length $l$), the sliding wire (length $l$), and the top and bottom horizontal segments (each of length $vt$).

The total length of the conducting loop at time $t$ is:

$L = l + l + vt + vt = 2(l + vt)$

Since all wires have a resistance of $r$ per unit length, the total resistance of the circuit at time $t$ is:

$R = Lr = 2r(l + vt)$

The induced current $I$ in the circuit is:

$I = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{Blv}{2r(l + vt)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire $PQ$ with mass $m$ and resistance $r$ is free to slide on smooth, horizontal parallel rails separated by a distance $l$. The resistance of the rails is considered negligible. A uniform magnetic field $B$ exists in the rectangular region, while a resistance $R$ connects the rails outside the field region. At $t = 0$, the wire $PQ$ is given a push to the right with a speed $v_0$. Determine the acceleration of the wire at an instant when its speed is $v$.
questions.solution:
When the wire $PQ$ moves with a velocity $v$, the motional electromotive force (emf) induced across its ends is given by:

$E = B l v$

The total resistance of the closed circuit is the sum of the resistance of the wire and the external resistance:

$R_{eq} = R + r$

The induced current flowing through the circuit is:

$I = \dfrac{E}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{B l v}{R + r}$

The magnetic force acting on the current-carrying wire $PQ$ in the magnetic field is:

$F = I l B = \left( \dfrac{B l v}{R + r} \right) l B = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{R + r}$

According to Lenz's law, the direction of the induced current is such that the magnetic force opposes the motion of the wire. Since the wire is moving to the right, the magnetic force acts towards the left.

The acceleration of the wire is:

$a = \dfrac{F}{m} = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{m(R + r)}$ towards the left.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As depicted in the figure, a wire $PQ$ of mass $m$ and resistance $r$ can slide on smooth, horizontal parallel rails separated by a distance $l$. The resistance of the rails is negligible. A uniform magnetic field $B$ exists in the rectangular region, and a resistance $R$ connects the rails outside the field region. At $t = 0$, the wire $PQ$ is pushed towards the right with a speed $v_0$. Determine the current in the loop at an instant when the speed of the wire $PQ$ is $v$.
questions.solution:
When the wire $PQ$ moves with a speed $v$ in a uniform magnetic field $B$, a motional electromotive force (emf) is induced across its ends.

The magnitude of this induced emf is given by:
$\varepsilon = Blv$

The total resistance of the closed loop is the sum of the resistance of the wire $PQ$ and the external resistance $R$.
Total resistance $= R + r$

The induced current $I$ in the loop at that instant is:
$I = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{Blv}{R + r}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Having a mass $m$ and resistance $r$, a wire $PQ$ can slide on smooth, horizontal parallel rails separated by a distance $l$. The rails have negligible resistance. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the rectangular region, and a resistance $R$ connects the rails outside the field region. At $t = 0$, the wire $PQ$ is projected towards the right with a speed $v_0$. Find the velocity $v$ of the wire as a function of its displacement $x$.
questions.solution:
When the wire moves with velocity $v$, the motional emf induced in it is given by:

$E = Bvl$

The total resistance of the circuit is $R_{eq} = R + r$. The induced current is:

$I = \dfrac{E}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{Bvl}{R + r}$

The magnetic force acting on the wire opposes its motion (by Lenz's law) and is given by:

$F = -IlB = -\left(\dfrac{Bvl}{R + r}\right)lB = -\dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{R + r}$

Using Newton's second law, $F = ma = mv\dfrac{dv}{dx}$:

$mv\dfrac{dv}{dx} = -\dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{R + r}$

Canceling $v$ from both sides:

$dv = -\dfrac{B^2 l^2}{m(R + r)} dx$

Integrating both sides with initial conditions $v = v_0$ at $x = 0$:

$\int_{v_0}^{v} dv = -\dfrac{B^2 l^2}{m(R + r)} \int_{0}^{x} dx$

$v - v_0 = -\dfrac{B^2 l^2 x}{m(R + r)}$

$v = v_0 - \dfrac{B^2 l^2 x}{m(R + r)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire $PQ$ of mass $m$ and resistance $r$ is capable of sliding on smooth, horizontal parallel rails separated by a distance $l$. The rails' resistance is negligible. A uniform magnetic field $B$ exists in the rectangular region, and a resistance $R$ connects the rails outside the field region. At $t = 0$, the wire $PQ$ is pushed towards the right with a speed $v_0$. Determine the maximum distance the wire will move.
questions.solution:
When the wire moves with velocity $v$, the motional emf induced is given by:

$\varepsilon = B l v$

The total resistance of the circuit is $R_{eq} = R + r$.
The induced current in the circuit is:

$i = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{B l v}{R + r}$

The magnetic force acting on the wire opposes its motion and is given by:

$F = -i l B = -\left( \dfrac{B l v}{R + r} \right) l B = -\dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{R + r}$

Using Newton's second law, $F = m a = m v \dfrac{dv}{dx}$:

$m v \dfrac{dv}{dx} = -\dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{R + r}$

$m \dfrac{dv}{dx} = -\dfrac{B^2 l^2}{R + r}$

$dv = -\dfrac{B^2 l^2}{m (R + r)} dx$

Integrating both sides from the initial state ($x = 0, v = v_0$) to the final state ($x = x_{\max}, v = 0$):

$\int_{v_0}^{0} dv = -\dfrac{B^2 l^2}{m (R + r)} \int_{0}^{x_{\max}} dx$

$0 - v_0 = -\dfrac{B^2 l^2}{m (R + r)} x_{\max}$

$x_{\max} = \dfrac{m v_0 (R + r)}{B^2 l^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rectangular wire frame $abcd$ has dimensions $32 \text{ cm} \times 8.0 \text{ cm}$ and a total resistance of $2.0 \text{ }\Omega$. By applying a force of $3.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N}$, it is pulled out of a magnetic field $B = 0.020 \text{ T}$ as shown in the figure. It is observed that the frame travels with a constant speed. Find the potential difference between the points $a$ and $b$.
questions.solution:
When the rectangular wire frame is pulled out of the magnetic field with a constant speed, the applied force $F$ is balanced by the magnetic force $F_m$ acting on the side $ab$ (since side $cd$ is outside the magnetic field).

The magnetic force is given by:
$F_m = I l B$

Since the frame moves with a constant speed, $F = F_m$. We can find the induced current $I$ in the loop:
$I = \dfrac{F}{l B}$

From the given figure, the length of the side $ab$ is $l = 8.0 \text{ cm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$.
Substituting the given values:
$I = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^{-5}}{0.08 \times 0.020} = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^{-5}}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.02 \text{ A}$

The total resistance of the frame is $R = 2.0 \text{ }\Omega$, and its total perimeter is $2 \times (32 + 8.0) = 80 \text{ cm}$.
Assuming the wire has a uniform cross-section, the resistance is proportional to the length. The resistance of the side $ab$ is:
$R_{ab} = \dfrac{8.0 \text{ cm}}{80 \text{ cm}} \times 2.0 \text{ }\Omega = 0.2 \text{ }\Omega$

The resistance of the remaining part of the frame (the external circuit for the source $ab$) is:
$R_{ext} = R - R_{ab} = 2.0 - 0.2 = 1.8 \text{ }\Omega$

The side $ab$ acts as a seat of electromotive force (emf). The potential difference between points $a$ and $b$ is the voltage across the external resistance $R_{ext}$:
$V_{ab} = I \times R_{ext}$
$V_{ab} = 0.02 \text{ A} \times 1.8 \text{ }\Omega = 0.036 \text{ V} = 3.6 \times 10^{-2} \text{ V}$

Answer: $3.6 \times 10^{-2} \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rectangular wire frame $abcd$ has dimensions $32 \text{ cm} \times 8.0 \text{ cm}$ and a total resistance of $2.0 \text{ }\Omega$. By applying a force of $3.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N}$, it is pulled out of a magnetic field $B = 0.020 \text{ T}$ as shown in the figure. It is observed that the frame travels with a constant speed. Determine the emf induced in the loop.
questions.solution:
Since the frame moves with a constant speed, the net force acting on it is zero. Thus, the applied force $F$ is balanced by the magnetic force $F_m$ acting on the side of the frame that is inside the magnetic field.

From the figure, the side $ab$ is perpendicular to the direction of motion and is inside the magnetic field. Based on the proportions in the figure, its length is $l = 8.0 \text{ cm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$.

The magnetic force is given by:
$F_m = I l B$
where $I$ is the induced current in the loop.

Equating the applied force to the magnetic force:
$F = I l B$
$3.2 \times 10^{-5} = I \times 0.08 \times 0.020$

Solving for the induced current $I$:
$I = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^{-5}}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ A}$

The induced emf $\varepsilon$ is given by Ohm's law:
$\varepsilon = I R$
$\varepsilon = (2.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ A}) \times (2.0 \text{ }\Omega) = 4.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rectangular wire frame $abcd$ has dimensions $32 \text{ cm} \times 8.0 \text{ cm}$ and a total resistance of $2.0 \text{ }\Omega$. By applying a force of $3.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N}$, it is pulled out of a magnetic field $B = 0.020 \text{ T}$ as shown in the figure. It is observed that the frame travels with a constant speed. Find this constant speed.
questions.solution:
When the rectangular frame is pulled out of the magnetic field with a constant velocity $v$, an emf is induced in the arm $ab$ which is still inside the magnetic field.

The induced motional emf is given by:
$E = B l v$
where $l = 8.0 \text{ cm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$ is the length of the arm $ab$ perpendicular to the velocity.

The induced current in the frame is:
$I = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{B l v}{R}$

The magnetic force opposing the motion of the frame acts on the arm $ab$ and is given by:
$F_m = I l B = \left(\dfrac{B l v}{R}\right) l B = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{R}$

Since the frame travels with a constant speed, the net force on it must be zero. Therefore, the applied force $F$ must be equal in magnitude to the magnetic force $F_m$:
$F = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{R}$

Rearranging for $v$, we get:
$v = \dfrac{F R}{B^2 l^2}$

Substituting the given values:
$F = 3.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N}$
$R = 2.0 \text{ }\Omega$
$B = 0.020 \text{ T}$
$l = 0.08 \text{ m}$

$v = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.0}{(0.020)^2 \times (0.08)^2}$

$v = \dfrac{6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \times 10^{-4} \times 64 \times 10^{-4}}$

$v = \dfrac{6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{256 \times 10^{-8}}$

$v = \dfrac{6400 \times 10^{-8}}{256 \times 10^{-8}} = 25 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rectangular wire frame $abcd$ has dimensions $32 \text{ cm} \times 8.0 \text{ cm}$ and a total resistance of $2.0 \text{ }\Omega$. By applying a force of $3.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N}$, it is pulled out of a magnetic field $B = 0.020 \text{ T}$ as shown in the figure. It is observed that the frame travels with a constant speed. Determine the potential difference between the points $c$ and $d$.
questions.solution:
Given:
Dimensions of the rectangular frame $= 32 \text{ cm} \times 8.0 \text{ cm}$
Total resistance of the frame, $R = 2.0 \text{ }\Omega$
Applied force, $F = 3.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N}$
Magnetic field, $B = 0.020 \text{ T}$

The total perimeter of the frame is:
$p = 2 \times (32 + 8.0) = 80 \text{ cm} = 0.8 \text{ m}$

Let the length of the side $ab$ (and $cd$) be $l$.
Since the frame is pulled out of the magnetic field with a constant speed, the net force on it is zero. The applied force $F$ is balanced by the magnetic force acting on the current-carrying side $ab$ (which is inside the magnetic field).
$F = I l B \Rightarrow I = \dfrac{F}{l B}$

The resistance of the frame is uniformly distributed along its length. The resistance of the side $cd$ is:
$R_{cd} = \dfrac{l}{p} R$

Since the side $cd$ is outside the magnetic field, there is no motional emf induced in it. The potential difference between points $c$ and $d$ is simply the ohmic voltage drop across the resistance $R_{cd}$:
$V_{cd} = I R_{cd}$

Substituting the expressions for $I$ and $R_{cd}$:
$V_{cd} = \left(\dfrac{F}{l B}\right) \left(\dfrac{l}{p} R\right) = \dfrac{F R}{B p}$

Notice that the length $l$ cancels out. Now, substituting the given values:
$V_{cd} = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.0}{0.020 \times 0.8}$

$V_{cd} = \dfrac{6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{0.016} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$

Answer: $4.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire $ab$ of length $l$, mass $m$, and resistance $R$ slides on a smooth, thick pair of metallic rails connected at the bottom, as shown in the figure. The plane of the rails forms an angle $ heta$ with the horizontal. A vertically directed magnetic field $B$ exists in the region. If the wire slides on the rails at a constant speed $v$, determine the magnetic field $B$.
questions.solution:
The wire moves down the inclined plane with a constant speed $v$. The horizontal component of its velocity, which is perpendicular to the vertically directed magnetic field $B$, is $v \cos\theta$.

The motional EMF induced in the wire is given by:

$E = B l (v \cos\theta)$

The induced current $I$ in the circuit is:

$I = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{B l v \cos\theta}{R}$

The magnetic force acting on the current-carrying wire is horizontal and its magnitude is:

$F_m = I l B$

The component of this magnetic force along the inclined plane opposes the downward motion and is given by:

$F_{\text{up}} = F_m \cos\theta = I l B \cos\theta$

For the wire to slide at a constant speed, the net force along the incline must be zero. Therefore, the component of gravitational force down the incline must be balanced by the magnetic force component up the incline:

$m g \sin\theta = I l B \cos\theta$

Substituting the expression for $I$ into the equation:

$m g \sin\theta = \left( \dfrac{B l v \cos\theta}{R} \right) l B \cos\theta$

$m g \sin\theta = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v \cos^2\theta}{R}$

Rearranging to solve for $B$:

$B^2 = \dfrac{m g R \sin\theta}{v l^2 \cos^2\theta}$

$B = \sqrt{\dfrac{m g R \sin\theta}{v l^2 \cos^2\theta}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the arrangement depicted in the figure. The wires $P_1 Q_1$ and $P_2 Q_2$ are forced to slide along the rails at an identical speed of $5 \text{ cm s}^{-1}$. Determine the electric current flowing through the $19 \text{ }\Omega$ resistor if both wires travel towards the right.
questions.solution:
The motional electromotive force (emf) induced in a wire of length $l$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a magnetic field $B$ is given by:

$E = Bvl$

Given values are:
$B = 1.0 \text{ T}$
$l = 4 \text{ cm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
$v = 5 \text{ cm s}^{-1} = 0.05 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

Substituting these values, the induced emf in each wire is:
$E = 1.0 \times 0.04 \times 0.05 = 0.002 \text{ V} = 2 \text{ mV}$

Since both wires $P_1 Q_1$ and $P_2 Q_2$ are moving in the same direction (towards the right), the induced emfs in both wires are in the same direction. They act as two identical batteries connected in parallel across the rails.

For two identical batteries in parallel, the equivalent emf is equal to the emf of a single battery:
$E_{eq} = E = 2 \text{ mV}$

The internal resistance of each wire is $r = 2 \text{ }\Omega$. Since they are in parallel, the equivalent internal resistance $r_{eq}$ is:
$r_{eq} = \dfrac{r_1 r_2}{r_1 + r_2} = \dfrac{2 \times 2}{2 + 2} = 1 \text{ }\Omega$

This equivalent battery is connected in series with the external resistor $R = 19 \text{ }\Omega$. The total resistance of the circuit is:
$R_{total} = R + r_{eq} = 19 + 1 = 20 \text{ }\Omega$

The electric current $I$ flowing through the $19 \text{ }\Omega$ resistor is:
$I = \dfrac{E_{eq}}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{2 \text{ mV}}{20 \text{ }\Omega} = 0.1 \text{ mA}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the arrangement depicted in the figure. The wires $P_1 Q_1$ and $P_2 Q_2$ are forced to slide along the rails at an identical speed of $5 \text{ cm s}^{-1}$. Determine the electric current flowing through the $19 \text{ }\Omega$ resistor if $P_1 Q_1$ travels towards the left while $P_2 Q_2$ travels towards the right.
questions.solution:
The motional electromotive force (EMF) induced in a wire moving through a magnetic field is given by $E = Bvl$.
Given:
Magnetic field $B = 1.0 \text{ T}$
Speed of wires $v = 5 \text{ cm s}^{-1} = 0.05 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
Length of wires $l = 4 \text{ cm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$

The magnitude of the induced EMF in each wire is:
$E = 1.0 \times 0.05 \times 0.04 = 0.002 \text{ V} = 2 \text{ mV}$

Now, we determine the direction of the induced EMF using the right-hand rule (or $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$):
For wire $P_1 Q_1$ moving towards the left, $\vec{v}$ is left and $\vec{B}$ is into the page. The force on positive charges is directed downwards (from $P_1$ to $Q_1$). Thus, $Q_1$ acts as the positive terminal, and $P_1$ as the negative terminal. The equivalent battery has $E_1 = 2 \text{ mV}$ with the positive terminal at the bottom rail.

For wire $P_2 Q_2$ moving towards the right, $\vec{v}$ is right and $\vec{B}$ is into the page. The force on positive charges is directed upwards (from $Q_2$ to $P_2$). Thus, $P_2$ acts as the positive terminal, and $Q_2$ as the negative terminal. The equivalent battery has $E_2 = 2 \text{ mV}$ with the positive terminal at the top rail.

Let the potential of the bottom rail be $0 \text{ V}$ and the potential of the top rail be $V$. We can apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the top rail. The sum of currents leaving the top node must be zero:
$\dfrac{V - (-E_1)}{R_1} + \dfrac{V - E_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V}{R} = 0$

Substitute the given values ($E_1 = 2 \text{ mV}$, $E_2 = 2 \text{ mV}$, $R_1 = 2 \text{ }\Omega$, $R_2 = 2 \text{ }\Omega$, $R = 19 \text{ }\Omega$):
$\dfrac{V + 2}{2} + \dfrac{V - 2}{2} + \dfrac{V}{19} = 0$

$\left(\dfrac{V}{2} + 1\right) + \left(\dfrac{V}{2} - 1\right) + \dfrac{V}{19} = 0$

$V + \dfrac{V}{19} = 0 \Rightarrow V = 0 \text{ V}$

Since the potential difference across the $19 \text{ }\Omega$ resistor is zero, the electric current flowing through it is also zero.

Answer: zero

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a metallic wire of resistance $0.20 \text{ }\Omega$ slides on a horizontal, U-shaped metallic rail. The separation between the parallel arms is $20 \text{ cm}$. When the wire is slid at a rate of $20 \text{ cm s}^{-1}$, an electric current of $2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{A}$ passes through it. Determine the dip at the place, given that the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field is $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$.
questions.solution:
The motional EMF induced in the sliding wire is due to the vertical component of the Earth's magnetic field, as the wire moves in a horizontal plane.

The induced EMF is given by:

$\varepsilon = B_V l v$

where $B_V$ is the vertical component of the magnetic field, $l$ is the length of the wire, and $v$ is its velocity.

According to Ohm's law, the induced current is:

$I = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{B_V l v}{R}$

Rearranging for $B_V$:

$B_V = \dfrac{I R}{l v}$

Given values are:
$I = 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}$
$R = 0.20 \text{ }\Omega$
$l = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
$v = 20 \text{ cm s}^{-1} = 0.2 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

Substituting these values:

$B_V = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.20}{0.2 \times 0.2}$

$B_V = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{-7}}{0.04} = 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

The angle of dip $\delta$ is related to the horizontal component $B_H$ and vertical component $B_V$ by:

$\tan \delta = \dfrac{B_V}{B_H}$

Given $B_H = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$, we have:

$\tan \delta = \dfrac{10^{-5}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{1}{3}$

$\delta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As depicted in the figure, the wires $P_1 Q_1$ and $P_2 Q_2$ slide along the rails at an identical speed of $5 \text{ cm s}^{-1}$. Assuming the $19 \text{ }\Omega$ resistor is disconnected, determine the current flowing through $P_2 Q_2$ when both wires travel towards the right.
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in a moving wire is given by $e = Bvl$.

For both wires $P_1 Q_1$ and $P_2 Q_2$, the induced emf is:
$e = 1.0 \times 0.04 \times 0.05 = 0.002 \text{ V} = 2 \text{ mV}$

Since both wires are moving in the same direction with the same speed, the induced emfs in both wires are equal in magnitude and have the same polarity (higher potential at $P_1$ and $P_2$).

With the $19 \text{ }\Omega$ resistor disconnected, the two wires form a closed loop $P_1 P_2 Q_2 Q_1$. In this loop, the two emfs oppose each other.

The net emf in the loop is $e_{\text{net}} = e - e = 0$.

Alternatively, since both wires move at the same velocity, the distance between them remains constant. Thus, the area of the loop and the magnetic flux through it remain constant ($\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = 0$), resulting in no induced current.

Therefore, the current flowing through $P_2 Q_2$ is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, the wire $PQ$ possesses negligible resistance and is made to slide on the three rails at a constant speed of $5 \text{ cm s}^{-1}$. Determine the current in the $10 \text{ }\Omega$ resistor when the switch $S$ is thrown to the middle rail.
questions.solution:
When the switch $S$ is connected to the middle rail, the closed circuit consists of the top rail, the middle rail, the $10 \text{ }\Omega$ resistor, and the upper segment of the sliding wire $PQ$.

The effective length $l$ of the wire $PQ$ in this circuit is the distance between the top and middle rails:

$l = 2 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$

The motional emf $E$ induced in this segment is given by:

$E = Blv$

Substituting the given values ($B = 1.0 \text{ T}$, $v = 5 \text{ cm s}^{-1} = 0.05 \text{ m s}^{-1}$):

$E = 1.0 \times 0.02 \times 0.05 = 10^{-3} \text{ V}$

The current $I$ in the $10 \text{ }\Omega$ resistor is:

$I = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{10^{-3}}{10} = 10^{-4} \text{ A}$

$I = 0.1 \text{ mA}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the configuration shown in the figure, the wire $PQ$ has negligible resistance and slides across the three rails at a constant speed of $5 \text{ cm s}^{-1}$. Calculate the current in the $10 \text{ }\Omega$ resistor when the switch $S$ is thrown to the bottom rail.
questions.solution:
When the switch $S$ is thrown to the bottom rail, the active circuit loop is formed by the top rail, the bottom rail, the wire $PQ$, and the $10\ \Omega$ resistor.

The effective length of the wire $PQ$ that moves in the magnetic field and forms part of the circuit is the distance between the top and bottom rails:

$l = 2\text{ cm} + 2\text{ cm} = 4\text{ cm} = 0.04\text{ m}$

The motional electromotive force (EMF) induced in the wire is given by:

$\varepsilon = Bvl$

Substituting the given values ($B = 1.0\text{ T}$, $v = 5\text{ cm s}^{-1} = 0.05\text{ m s}^{-1}$, $l = 0.04\text{ m}$):

$\varepsilon = 1.0 \times 0.05 \times 0.04 = 0.002\text{ V}$

The current $I$ in the $10\ \Omega$ resistor is:

$I = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{0.002}{10} = 0.0002\text{ A}$

$I = 0.2\text{ mA}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a current generator $I_g$ delivers a constant current $i$ to the circuit. The wire $cd$ is held fixed, whereas the wire $ab$ is made to slide on the smooth, thick rails with a constant velocity $v$ towards the right. Each of these wires has a resistance $r$. Determine the current passing through the wire $cd$.
questions.solution:
Let the current passing through the wire $cd$ be $i_1$ and through the wire $ab$ be $i_2$. Both currents flow from the top rail to the bottom rail.

According to Kirchhoff's current law, the total current $i$ from the generator splits into the two branches:
$i = i_1 + i_2$

The wire $ab$ moves with a constant velocity $v$ in a uniform magnetic field $B$ directed into the plane of the paper. This induces a motional EMF in the wire $ab$ given by:
$E = Bvl$

Using the right-hand rule for $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$, the induced EMF is directed from $b$ to $a$ (upwards). Thus, the wire $ab$ acts as a voltage source with its positive terminal at $a$.

Since the wires $cd$ and $ab$ are connected in parallel across the smooth, thick rails, the potential difference across them must be equal.
For the branch $cd$, the potential difference is:
$V = i_1 r$

For the branch $ab$, the potential difference is the sum of the voltage drop due to its resistance and the opposing induced EMF:
$V = i_2 r + Bvl$

Equating the potential differences across the two branches:
$i_1 r = i_2 r + Bvl$

Substitute $i_2 = i - i_1$ into the equation:
$i_1 r = (i - i_1) r + Bvl$

$i_1 r = ir - i_1 r + Bvl$

$2i_1 r = ir + Bvl$

$i_1 = \dfrac{ir + Bvl}{2r}$

Answer: $\dfrac{ir + Blv}{2r}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As depicted in the figure, a current generator $I_g$ drives a constant current $i$ through the circuit. A wire $ab$, having mass $m$ and length $l$, is capable of sliding along the smooth horizontal rails linked to $I_g$. The complete arrangement is positioned within a vertical magnetic field $B$. Determine the velocity of the wire as a function of time $t$.
questions.solution:
From the given circuit diagram, the constant current $i$ flows through the wire $ab$ from $a$ to $b$.

The uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is directed into the plane of the paper.

The magnetic force acting on the current-carrying wire $ab$ is given by $\vec{F} = i(\vec{l} \times \vec{B})$.

The magnitude of this force is $F = ilB\sin(90^\circ) = ilB$.

Using the right-hand rule or Fleming's left-hand rule, the direction of this force is towards the right, which is away from the generator.

Since the horizontal rails are smooth, the net force on the wire is $F = ilB$.

The acceleration of the wire is $a = \dfrac{F}{m} = \dfrac{ilB}{m}$.

Assuming the wire starts from rest at $t=0$, its velocity at time $t$ can be found using the first equation of motion, $v = u + at$:

$v = 0 + \left(\dfrac{ilB}{m}\right)t = \dfrac{ilBt}{m}$

The direction of the velocity is the same as the direction of the force, i.e., away from the generator.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A constant current $i$ is delivered by a current generator $I_g$ through a circuit featuring smooth rails and a wire $ab$ of length $l$ and mass $m$ capable of sliding on the rails. This system is maintained vertically within a uniform horizontal magnetic field $B$ oriented perpendicular to the plane of the rails, as shown in the figure. The wire is observed to stay in equilibrium. Should the wire $ab$ be replaced by a new wire of double its mass, how much time will it take to fall through a distance equal to its length?
questions.solution:
For the initial wire of mass $m$ and length $l$ in equilibrium, the downward gravitational force is balanced by the upward magnetic force.
The magnetic force on the current-carrying wire is given by $F_m = ilB$.
At equilibrium:
$ilB = mg$

When the wire is replaced by a new wire of double its mass ($2m$), the gravitational force becomes $2mg$.
Since the current $i$, length $l$, and magnetic field $B$ remain constant, the upward magnetic force remains the same:
$F_m = ilB = mg$

The net downward force acting on the new wire is:
$F_{\text{net}} = 2mg - F_m = 2mg - mg = mg$

The downward acceleration $a$ of the new wire is:
$a = \dfrac{F_{\text{net}}}{\text{Mass}} = \dfrac{mg}{2m} = \dfrac{g}{2}$

We need to find the time $t$ taken by the wire to fall through a distance equal to its length $l$.
Using the second equation of motion, $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$, with initial velocity $u = 0$ and distance $s = l$:
$l = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{g}{2}\right) t^2$

$l = \dfrac{g}{4} t^2$

$t^2 = \dfrac{4l}{g}$

$t = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rectangular wire-frame possesses a width $d$, mass $m$, resistance $R$, and a large length. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is situated to the left of the frame. At $t = 0$, a constant force $F$ begins pushing the frame into the magnetic field. After a certain period, the frame will travel with a constant velocity until it fully enters the magnetic field. Determine this terminal velocity $v_0$.
questions.solution:
When the rectangular frame enters the magnetic field with velocity $v$, an induced electromotive force (emf) is generated across its width $d$.

The motional emf is given by:
$e = B d v$

The induced current in the frame is:
$i = \dfrac{e}{R} = \dfrac{B d v}{R}$

According to Lenz's law, the magnetic force $F_m$ acting on the current-carrying wire opposes the motion of the frame. The magnitude of this force is:
$F_m = i d B = \left(\dfrac{B d v}{R}\right) d B = \dfrac{B^2 d^2 v}{R}$

The net force acting on the frame is:
$F_{net} = F - F_m = F - \dfrac{B^2 d^2 v}{R}$

The frame reaches a constant terminal velocity $v_0$ when the net force becomes zero ($F_{net} = 0$):
$F - \dfrac{B^2 d^2 v_0}{R} = 0$

Solving for $v_0$, we get:
$v_0 = \dfrac{F R}{d^2 B^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rectangular wire-frame having a width $d$, mass $m$, resistance $R$, and a large length is positioned such that a uniform magnetic field $B$ exists to its left. At $t = 0$, a constant force $F$ starts pushing the frame into the magnetic field. If $v_0$ is the constant terminal velocity the frame eventually attains, evaluate the expression for the velocity $v$ of the frame at any time $t$ before it fully enters the field.
questions.solution:
Let the velocity of the frame at time $t$ be $v$.

The induced motional emf in the frame is $\varepsilon = B d v$.

The induced current is $I = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{B d v}{R}$.

The magnetic force opposing the motion of the frame is $F_m = I d B = \dfrac{B^2 d^2 v}{R}$.

The net force acting on the frame is $F_{\text{net}} = F - F_m = F - \dfrac{B^2 d^2 v}{R}$.

Using Newton's second law, $m \dfrac{dv}{dt} = F - \dfrac{B^2 d^2 v}{R}$.

When the frame attains the constant terminal velocity $v_0$, the net force becomes zero, i.e., $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$.

$F - \dfrac{B^2 d^2 v_0}{R} = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{B^2 d^2}{R} = \dfrac{F}{v_0}$.

Substituting this back into the equation of motion, we get:

$m \dfrac{dv}{dt} = F - \left(\dfrac{F}{v_0}\right) v = F \left(1 - \dfrac{v}{v_0}\right)$.

Rearranging the variables, we have:

$\dfrac{dv}{1 - \dfrac{v}{v_0}} = \dfrac{F}{m} dt$.

Integrating both sides with the initial condition $v = 0$ at $t = 0$:

$\int_{0}^{v} \dfrac{dv}{1 - \dfrac{v}{v_0}} = \int_{0}^{t} \dfrac{F}{m} dt$

$-v_0 \ln\left(1 - \dfrac{v}{v_0}\right) = \dfrac{F}{m} t$

$\ln\left(1 - \dfrac{v}{v_0}\right) = -\dfrac{F t}{m v_0}$

$1 - \dfrac{v}{v_0} = e^{-\dfrac{F t}{m v_0}}$

$v = v_0 \left(1 - e^{-\dfrac{F t}{m v_0}}\right)$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rectangular wire-frame of width $d$, mass $m$, and resistance $R$ has a large length. As shown in the figure, a uniform magnetic field $B$ is present to the left of the frame. At $t = 0$, a constant force $F$ begins pushing the frame into the magnetic field region. Determine the acceleration of the frame once its speed reaches $v$.
questions.solution:
When the rectangular wire-frame enters the magnetic field with velocity $v$, an induced motional emf is generated in its leading edge of length $d$.

The induced emf is given by:
$\varepsilon = Bvd$

The induced current in the frame is:
$I = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{Bvd}{R}$

This current flowing through the leading edge experiences a magnetic force opposing the motion of the frame (according to Lenz's law). The magnitude of this magnetic force is:
$F_m = IdB = \left(\dfrac{Bvd}{R}\right)dB = \dfrac{B^2 d^2 v}{R}$

The net force acting on the frame is the applied force $F$ minus the opposing magnetic force $F_m$:
$F_{net} = F - F_m = F - \dfrac{B^2 d^2 v}{R}$

Using Newton's second law, the acceleration $a$ of the frame is:
$a = \dfrac{F_{net}}{m} = \dfrac{F - \dfrac{B^2 d^2 v}{R}}{m}$

$a = \dfrac{RF - v d^2 B^2}{mR}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A smooth pair of thick metallic rails is connected across a battery of emf $\mathcal{E}$ with negligible internal resistance. A wire $ab$ having length $l$ and resistance $r$ can slide smoothly on these rails. The whole system is situated in a horizontal plane and placed in a uniform vertical magnetic field $B$. At a certain instant $t$, the wire is imparted a small velocity $v$ towards the right. Determine the magnetic force acting on the wire at this instant.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is directed vertically outwards from the plane of the paper (indicated by dots).

The wire $ab$ moves with velocity $\vec{v}$ towards the right. The motional emf induced across the wire is given by $e = Bvl$.

The direction of the motional emf is given by $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$. Since $\vec{v}$ is towards the right ($\hat{i}$) and $\vec{B}$ is outwards ($\hat{k}$), the direction of positive charge flow is $\hat{i} \times \hat{k} = -\hat{j}$ (downwards, from $a$ to $b$). Thus, the motional emf acts as a source driving current from $a$ to $b$.

The battery has its positive terminal connected to the top rail, so it also drives current in the downward direction from $a$ to $b$ through the wire.

Since both the battery and the motional emf drive current in the same direction, the net emf in the circuit is $\mathcal{E}_{net} = \mathcal{E} + Bvl$.

The total current flowing through the wire is $I = \dfrac{\mathcal{E} + Bvl}{r}$, directed from $a$ to $b$.

The magnetic force acting on the wire is $\vec{F} = I(\vec{l} \times \vec{B})$.

The direction of current $\vec{l}$ is downwards ($-\hat{j}$) and $\vec{B}$ is outwards ($\hat{k}$). The direction of the force is $(-\hat{j}) \times \hat{k} = -\hat{i}$, which is towards the left.

The magnitude of the magnetic force is $F = IlB = \dfrac{lB}{r}(\mathcal{E} + vBl)$.

Thus, the magnetic force acting on the wire is $\dfrac{lB}{r}(\mathcal{E} + vBl)$ towards the left.

Answer: $\dfrac{lB}{r}(\mathcal{E} + vBl)$ towards the left

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A smooth pair of thick metallic rails is connected across a battery of emf $\mathcal{E}$ with negligible internal resistance. A wire $ab$ having length $l$ and resistance $r$ can slide smoothly on these rails. The whole system is situated in a horizontal plane and placed in a uniform vertical magnetic field $B$. At a certain instant $t$, the wire is imparted a small velocity $v$ towards the right. Determine the current in the wire at this instant and its direction.
questions.solution:
The battery has its positive terminal connected to the upper rail, so it drives a current through the wire from $a$ to $b$.

The wire $ab$ moves towards the right with velocity $v$ in a uniform magnetic field $B$ directed outwards (out of the plane of the paper). The motional emf induced in the wire is given by $e = Bvl$.

The direction of this induced emf can be found using the relation $\vec{E}_{ind} = \vec{v} \times \vec{B}$. Since $\vec{v}$ is towards the right and $\vec{B}$ is outwards, the cross product $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ points downwards, i.e., from $a$ to $b$. Alternatively, by Lenz's law, the outward magnetic flux through the loop is increasing, so the induced current must produce an inward magnetic field, which corresponds to a clockwise current (from $a$ to $b$ through the wire).

Since both the battery's emf $\mathcal{E}$ and the motional emf $Bvl$ drive current in the same direction (from $a$ to $b$), the total effective emf in the circuit is $\mathcal{E}_{net} = \mathcal{E} + Bvl$.

The total resistance of the circuit is $r$. Therefore, the current in the wire is:
$I = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}_{net}}{r} = \dfrac{1}{r}(\mathcal{E} + vBl)$

The direction of this current is from $a$ to $b$.

Answer: $\dfrac{1}{r}(\mathcal{E} + vBl)$ from $a$ to $b$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A smooth pair of thick metallic rails is connected across a battery of emf $\mathcal{E}$ with negligible internal resistance. A wire $ab$ having length $l$ and resistance $r$ can slide smoothly on these rails. The whole system is situated in a horizontal plane and placed in a uniform vertical magnetic field $B$. At a certain instant $t$, the wire is imparted a small velocity $v$ towards the right. After some time, the wire $ab$ will slide with a constant velocity. Determine this constant velocity.
questions.solution:
Let the velocity of the wire at any instant be $v'$.

The motional emf induced across the ends of the wire due to its motion in the magnetic field is $e = B l v'$.

The current in the circuit is given by the net emf divided by the resistance:

$I = \dfrac{\mathcal{E} - B l v'}{r}$

The magnetic force acting on the current-carrying wire is:

$F = I l B = \left( \dfrac{\mathcal{E} - B l v'}{r} \right) l B$

The wire will achieve a constant velocity when its acceleration is zero, which means the net magnetic force acting on it must be zero.

Setting $F = 0$ gives:

$\mathcal{E} - B l v' = 0$

$v' = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}}{B l}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\mathcal{E}}{Bl}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, a conducting wire $ab$ of length $l$, resistance $r$, and mass $m$ begins sliding down a smooth, vertical, thick pair of connected rails. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the space, directed perpendicular to the plane of the rails. Find the downward acceleration of the wire at an instant when its speed is $v$.
questions.solution:
When the wire moves downwards with speed $v$, the motional emf induced in it is:

$\varepsilon = Bvl$

The induced current in the closed loop is:

$i = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{r} = \dfrac{Bvl}{r}$

This current experiences an upward magnetic force that opposes the downward motion (by Lenz's law):

$F_m = ilB = \left(\dfrac{Bvl}{r}\right)lB = \dfrac{B^2l^2v}{r}$

The net downward force acting on the wire is:

$F_{net} = mg - F_m = mg - \dfrac{B^2l^2v}{r}$

The downward acceleration $a$ is given by $F_{net} = ma$:

$ma = mg - \dfrac{B^2l^2v}{r}$

$a = g - \dfrac{B^2l^2v}{mr}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, a conducting wire $ab$ of length $l$, resistance $r$, and mass $m$ begins sliding down a smooth, vertical, thick pair of connected rails. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the space, directed perpendicular to the plane of the rails. What are the magnitude and direction of the induced current in the wire at an instant when its speed is $v$?
questions.solution:
The motional EMF induced in the conducting wire $ab$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field $B$ is given by $E = Bvl$.

Since the rails are thick, their resistance is considered negligible. The total resistance of the closed circuit is simply the resistance of the wire, $r$.

The magnitude of the induced current is $I = \dfrac{E}{r} = \dfrac{Bvl}{r}$.

The direction of the induced current can be determined using Lenz's law. The magnetic field is directed outwards from the plane of the page. As the wire slides downwards, the area of the loop increases, which increases the outward magnetic flux.

To oppose this increase in flux, the induced current must produce an inward magnetic field. By the right-hand rule, this requires a clockwise current in the loop.

A clockwise current means the current flows from $b$ to $a$ through the wire $ab$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, a conducting wire $ab$ of length $l$, resistance $r$, and mass $m$ begins sliding down a smooth, vertical, thick pair of connected rails as shown in the figure. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the space, directed perpendicular to the plane of the rails. Determine the induced emf in the loop at an instant $t$ when the speed of the wire is $v$.
questions.solution:
Let the distance of the wire $ab$ from the top of the rails at any instant $t$ be $y$.

The area of the loop formed by the rails and the wire is $A = ly$.

The magnetic flux linked with the loop is given by:
$\Phi = BA = Bly$

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the magnitude of the induced emf is:
$\varepsilon = \left| \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \right| = \dfrac{d}{dt}(Bly)$

Since the magnetic field $B$ and length $l$ are constant, we get:
$\varepsilon = Bl \dfrac{dy}{dt}$

Given that the speed of the wire is $v = \dfrac{dy}{dt}$, the induced emf becomes:
$\varepsilon = Bvl$

Alternatively, the motional emf across a conductor of length $l$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field $B$ is directly given by the scalar triple product of velocity, magnetic field, and length vectors, which yields $\varepsilon = Bvl$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, a conducting wire $ab$ of length $l$, resistance $r$, and mass $m$ begins sliding down a smooth, vertical, thick pair of connected rails. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the space, directed perpendicular to the plane of the rails. Let $v_m$ be the terminal velocity of the wire. Find the velocity of the wire as a function of time $t$.
questions.solution:
Let the velocity of the wire at time $t$ be $v$.

The motional emf induced in the wire is given by $E = B l v$.

The induced current in the circuit is $i = \dfrac{E}{r} = \dfrac{B l v}{r}$.

The magnetic force acting on the wire opposes its downward motion and is given by $F_m = i l B = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{r}$.

The equation of motion for the wire is:

$m \dfrac{dv}{dt} = mg - F_m = mg - \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{r}$

When the wire reaches its terminal velocity $v_m$, its acceleration becomes zero, so $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$.

$mg = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v_m}{r} \Rightarrow \dfrac{B^2 l^2}{r} = \dfrac{mg}{v_m}$

Substituting this back into the equation of motion, we get:

$m \dfrac{dv}{dt} = mg - \left(\dfrac{mg}{v_m}\right)v$

$\dfrac{dv}{dt} = g\left(1 - \dfrac{v}{v_m}\right)$

Rearranging and integrating with initial condition $v = 0$ at $t = 0$:

$\int_{0}^{v} \dfrac{dv}{v_m - v} = \int_{0}^{t} \dfrac{g}{v_m} dt$

$-\left[ \ln(v_m - v) \right]_{0}^{v} = \dfrac{gt}{v_m}$

$\ln\left(\dfrac{v_m - v}{v_m}\right) = -\dfrac{gt}{v_m}$

$1 - \dfrac{v}{v_m} = e^{-gt / v_m}$

$v = v_m (1 - e^{-gt / v_m})$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, a conducting wire $ab$ of length $l$, resistance $r$, and mass $m$ begins sliding down a smooth, vertical, thick pair of connected rails. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the space, directed perpendicular to the plane of the rails. After a sufficient time, the wire travels with a constant velocity. Determine this terminal velocity $v_m$.
questions.solution:
Let the velocity of the wire at any instant be $v$.

The motional emf induced in the wire is given by:
$e = Bvl$

The induced current in the loop is:
$i = \dfrac{e}{r} = \dfrac{Bvl}{r}$

The magnetic force acting on the current-carrying wire opposes its downward motion and is given by:
$F_m = ilB = \left(\dfrac{Bvl}{r}\right)lB = \dfrac{B^2l^2v}{r}$

At terminal velocity $v_m$, the acceleration of the wire is zero. Therefore, the downward gravitational force is balanced by the upward magnetic force:
$mg = F_m$

$mg = \dfrac{B^2l^2v_m}{r}$

Solving for $v_m$, we get:
$v_m = \dfrac{mgr}{B^2l^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, a conducting wire $ab$ of length $l$, resistance $r$, and mass $m$ begins sliding down a smooth, vertical, thick pair of connected rails. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the space, directed perpendicular to the plane of the rails. Let $v_m$ be the terminal velocity of the wire. Determine the displacement of the wire as a function of time $t$.
questions.solution:
Let $v$ be the downward velocity of the wire at time $t$.

The motional emf induced in the wire is $\varepsilon = Bvl$.

The induced current in the circuit is $i = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{r} = \dfrac{Bvl}{r}$.

The magnetic force acting on the wire opposes its motion and is given by $F_m = ilB = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{r}$.

The net downward force on the wire is $F_{net} = mg - F_m$, so the equation of motion is:

$m \dfrac{dv}{dt} = mg - \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{r}$

At terminal velocity $v_m$, the acceleration $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$, so:

$mg = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v_m}{r} \Rightarrow \dfrac{B^2 l^2}{r} = \dfrac{mg}{v_m}$

Substituting this into the equation of motion:

$m \dfrac{dv}{dt} = mg - \dfrac{mg}{v_m} v$

$\dfrac{dv}{dt} = g \left(1 - \dfrac{v}{v_m}\right)$

$\dfrac{dv}{1 - \dfrac{v}{v_m}} = g dt$

Integrating both sides with initial condition $v(0) = 0$:

$\int_{0}^{v} \dfrac{dv}{1 - \dfrac{v}{v_m}} = \int_{0}^{t} g dt$

$-v_m \ln\left(1 - \dfrac{v}{v_m}\right) = gt$

$1 - \dfrac{v}{v_m} = e^{-gt/v_m}$

$v = v_m (1 - e^{-gt/v_m})$

Since $v = \dfrac{dy}{dt}$, the displacement $y$ is:

$y = \int_{0}^{t} v_m (1 - e^{-gt/v_m}) dt$

$y = \left[ v_m t - v_m \left( \dfrac{e^{-gt/v_m}}{-g/v_m} \right) \right]_{0}^{t}$

$y = \left[ v_m t + \dfrac{v_m^2}{g} e^{-gt/v_m} \right]_{0}^{t}$

$y = \left( v_m t + \dfrac{v_m^2}{g} e^{-gt/v_m} \right) - \left( 0 + \dfrac{v_m^2}{g} \right)$

$y = v_m t - \dfrac{v_m^2}{g} (1 - e^{-gt/v_m})$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting disc of radius $r$ rotates about its central axis with a small but constant angular velocity $\omega$. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is directed parallel to the rotation axis. Determine the motional emf induced between the centre and the periphery of the disc.
questions.solution:
Consider a small radial element of length $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the centre of the disc.

The linear velocity of this element is $v = x \omega$.

The motional emf induced across this small element is $d\varepsilon = B v dx = B (x \omega) dx$.

The total emf induced between the centre and the periphery is obtained by integrating $d\varepsilon$ from $x = 0$ to $x = r$:

$\varepsilon = \int_{0}^{r} B \omega x dx$

$\varepsilon = B \omega \left[ \dfrac{x^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{r}$

$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{2} \omega r^2 B$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bicycle rests on its stand oriented in the east-west direction, and its rear wheel rotates at an angular speed of $100$ revolutions per minute. Given that the length of each spoke is $30.0 \text{ cm}$ and the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field is $2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$, determine the emf induced between the axis and the outer end of a spoke. Neglect the centripetal force acting on the free electrons within the spoke.
questions.solution:
The bicycle is oriented in the east-west direction, so the plane of the rear wheel is in the east-west vertical plane.

The horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field, $B_H$, is directed north-south, which is perpendicular to the plane of the wheel.

The induced emf $e$ between the axis and the outer end of a spoke is given by the formula:

$e = \dfrac{1}{2} B_H \omega l^2$

Given:

$B_H = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

$\omega = 100 \text{ rev/min} = 100 \times \dfrac{2\pi}{60} \text{ rad/s} = \dfrac{10\pi}{3} \text{ rad/s}$

$l = 30.0 \text{ cm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$

Substituting the values:

$e = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (2.0 \times 10^{-5}) \times \left(\dfrac{10\pi}{3}\right) \times (0.3)^2$

$e = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \times \dfrac{10\pi}{3} \times 0.09$

$e = 3\pi \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:

$e \approx 3 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V} = 9.42 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$

This is approximately $9.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, a conducting wire $ab$ of length $l$, resistance $r$, and mass $m$ begins sliding down a smooth, vertical, thick pair of connected rails. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present in the space, directed perpendicular to the plane of the rails. After steady state is reached, what is the relationship between the rate of heat developed in the wire and the rate at which the gravitational potential energy decreases?
options: The rate of heat developed is twice the rate of potential energy decrease.
The rate of heat developed is zero.
The rate of heat developed is half the rate of potential energy decrease.
questions.solution:
When the wire slides down with a velocity $v$, the induced motional emf is $E = Bvl$.

The induced current in the circuit is $I = \dfrac{E}{r} = \dfrac{Bvl}{r}$.

The upward magnetic force acting on the wire is $F_m = IlB = \dfrac{B^2l^2v}{r}$.

At steady state, the wire moves with a constant terminal velocity $v_t$, so the net force is zero. Equating the downward gravitational force to the upward magnetic force:

$mg = \dfrac{B^2l^2v_t}{r}$

The rate of decrease of gravitational potential energy is:

$P_{grav} = mgv_t$

The rate of heat developed in the wire is:

$P_{heat} = I^2r = \left(\dfrac{Bv_tl}{r}\right)^2 r = \dfrac{B^2l^2v_t^2}{r}$

Substituting $mg = \dfrac{B^2l^2v_t}{r}$ into the expression for $P_{heat}$:

$P_{heat} = \left(\dfrac{B^2l^2v_t}{r}\right)v_t = mgv_t$

Therefore, $P_{heat} = P_{grav}$, meaning the rate of heat developed is exactly equal to the rate of potential energy decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a long, straight wire carries a current $i$ and a rod of length $l$ is coplanar with the wire and oriented perpendicular to it. The rod travels with a constant velocity $v$ in a direction parallel to the wire. The distance from the wire to the centre of the rod is $x$. Determine the motional emf induced in the rod.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by a long straight wire carrying current $i$ at a distance $r$ from it is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$

The direction of the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane containing the wire and the rod (into the page).

Consider a small element of the rod of length $dr$ at a distance $r$ from the wire. The motional emf induced in this small element moving with velocity $v$ is:

$d\varepsilon = B v dr$

Since the center of the rod is at a distance $x$ from the wire and its total length is $l$, the rod extends from $r = x - \dfrac{l}{2}$ to $r = x + \dfrac{l}{2}$.

The total motional emf induced in the rod is obtained by integrating $d\varepsilon$ over these limits:

$\varepsilon = \int_{x - l/2}^{x + l/2} \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi r} dr$

$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \left[ \ln r \right]_{x - l/2}^{x + l/2}$

$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left( \dfrac{x + l/2}{x - l/2} \right)$

$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left( \dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l} \right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a conducting disc rotates about its axis within a perpendicular magnetic field $B$. A resistor having resistance $R$ is linked between the centre and the rim. Evaluate the current passing through the resistor and determine if it enters or leaves the disc at the centre. The radius of the disc is $5.0 \text{ cm}$, its angular speed $\omega = 10 \text{ rad/s}$, $B = 0.40 \text{ T}$, and $R = 10 \text{ }\Omega$.
options: $1.0 \text{ mA}$, leaves the disc at the centre
$0.5 \text{ mA}$, leaves the disc at the centre
$0.5 \text{ mA}$, enters the disc at the centre
$1.0 \text{ mA}$, enters the disc at the centre
questions.solution:
The induced emf $E$ between the centre and the rim of a rotating conducting disc in a uniform magnetic field is given by:

$E = \dfrac{1}{2} B \omega r^2$

Given values are:
$B = 0.40 \text{ T}$
$\omega = 10 \text{ rad/s}$
$r = 5.0 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$

Substituting these values into the formula:

$E = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.40 \times 10 \times (0.05)^2$

$E = 2.0 \times 0.0025 = 0.005 \text{ V}$

The current $I$ passing through the resistor $R = 10 \text{ }\Omega$ is:

$I = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{0.005}{10} = 0.0005 \text{ A} = 0.5 \text{ mA}$

To determine the direction of the current, we use the Lorentz force on a positive charge $q$ inside the disc, which is $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.
The magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is directed into the page, and the velocity $\vec{v}$ of any point on the disc is tangential in the counter-clockwise direction. By the right-hand rule, the cross product $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ points radially inwards towards the centre.
This means positive charges are pushed towards the centre, making it at a higher potential compared to the rim.

In the external circuit (the resistor), current flows from the higher potential to the lower potential. Thus, the current flows from the centre to the rim through the resistor, which means it leaves the disc at the centre.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a region where the magnetic field is expressed as $\vec{B} = \hat{k} \dfrac{B_0}{L} y$, with $L$ being a constant length. A conducting rod of length $L$ is positioned along the Y-axis from the origin to the point $(0, L, 0)$. Determine the induced emf between the ends of the rod if it travels with a velocity $\vec{v} = v_0 \hat{i}$.
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in a small element $d\vec{l}$ of the rod is given by $d\varepsilon = (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$.

Here, $\vec{v} = v_0 \hat{i}$, $\vec{B} = \dfrac{B_0}{L} y \hat{k}$, and $d\vec{l} = dy \hat{j}$.

Evaluating the cross product:

$\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = (v_0 \hat{i}) \times \left( \dfrac{B_0}{L} y \hat{k} \right) = - \dfrac{B_0 v_0}{L} y \hat{j}$

The induced emf in the small element is:

$d\varepsilon = \left( - \dfrac{B_0 v_0}{L} y \hat{j} \right) \cdot (dy \hat{j}) = - \dfrac{B_0 v_0}{L} y dy$

The total induced emf is the magnitude of the integral of $d\varepsilon$ from $y = 0$ to $y = L$:

$\varepsilon = \left| \int_{0}^{L} - \dfrac{B_0 v_0}{L} y dy \right|$

$\varepsilon = \dfrac{B_0 v_0}{L} \left[ \dfrac{y^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{L}$

$\varepsilon = \dfrac{B_0 v_0}{L} \left( \dfrac{L^2}{2} \right) = \dfrac{B_0 v_0 L}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A straight, long wire carries a current $i$. Coplanar with the wire and perpendicular to it is a rod of length $l$. The ends of the rod slide on a pair of thick metallic rails laid parallel to the wire, and at one end the rails are connected by a resistor of resistance $R$. The distance of the wire from the centre of the rod is $x$. If the rod travels with a constant velocity $v$ in a direction parallel to the wire, determine the force needed to keep the rod sliding at a constant speed $v$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by the long straight wire at a distance $r$ from it is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$

Consider a small element of the rod of length $dr$ at a distance $r$ from the wire. The motional emf induced in this element as it moves with velocity $v$ parallel to the wire is:

$d\varepsilon = B v dr = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi r} dr$

The rod has a length $l$ and its centre is at a distance $x$ from the wire. Therefore, the ends of the rod are at distances $r_1 = x - \dfrac{l}{2}$ and $r_2 = x + \dfrac{l}{2}$ from the wire.

The total emf induced in the rod is obtained by integrating $d\varepsilon$ from $r_1$ to $r_2$:

$\varepsilon = \int_{x - l/2}^{x + l/2} \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi r} dr = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{x + l/2}{x - l/2}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right)$

The induced current in the circuit is:

$I_{ind} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi R} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right)$

To keep the rod moving at a constant speed, the external mechanical power supplied must be equal to the electrical power dissipated in the resistor (by conservation of energy):

$P_{mech} = P_{elec}$

$F v = \dfrac{\varepsilon^2}{R}$

$F = \dfrac{\varepsilon^2}{v R}$

Substituting the value of $\varepsilon$:

$F = \dfrac{1}{v R} \left[ \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right) \right]^2$

$F = \dfrac{v}{R} \left[ \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right) \right]^2$

Answer: $\dfrac{v}{R}\left[\dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right)\right]^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A straight, long wire carries a current $i$. Coplanar with the wire and perpendicular to it is a rod of length $l$. The ends of the rod slide on a pair of thick metallic rails laid parallel to the wire, and at one end the rails are connected by a resistor of resistance $R$. The distance of the wire from the centre of the rod is $x$. If the rod travels with a constant velocity $v$ in a direction parallel to the wire, determine the current in the resistance $R$.
questions.solution:
Consider an element of length $dr$ on the rod at a distance $r$ from the long straight wire.

The magnetic field at this distance is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$.

The motional emf induced in this small element as it moves with velocity $v$ parallel to the wire is $dE = B v dr$.

The total induced emf in the rod is obtained by integrating this expression from $r = x - \dfrac{l}{2}$ to $r = x + \dfrac{l}{2}$:

$E = \int_{x - l/2}^{x + l/2} \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi r} dr$

$E = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} [\ln r]_{x - l/2}^{x + l/2}$

$E = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{x + l/2}{x - l/2}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right)$

The induced current in the circuit with resistance $R$ is $I = \dfrac{E}{R}$.

$I = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi R} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right)$

Answer: $\dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi R} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A straight, long wire carries a current $i$. Coplanar with the wire and perpendicular to it is a rod of length $l$. The ends of the rod slide on a pair of thick metallic rails laid parallel to the wire, and at one end the rails are connected by a resistor of resistance $R$. The distance of the wire from the centre of the rod is $x$. If the rod travels with a constant velocity $v$ in a direction parallel to the wire, find the rate of heat developed in the resistor.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by the long straight current-carrying wire at a distance $r$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$.

The rod moves with a constant velocity $v$ parallel to the wire. The motional emf induced in a small element of length $dr$ of the rod at a distance $r$ from the wire is $dE = B v dr = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi r} dr$.

The total emf induced across the ends of the rod is obtained by integrating $dE$ from the nearer end to the farther end. Since the centre of the rod is at a distance $x$ and its length is $l$, the ends are at distances $x - \dfrac{l}{2}$ and $x + \dfrac{l}{2}$ from the wire.

$E = \int_{x - l/2}^{x + l/2} \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi r} dr = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} [\ln r]_{x - l/2}^{x + l/2}$

$E = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{x + l/2}{x - l/2}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right)$

The rate of heat developed in the resistor $R$ is given by $P = \dfrac{E^2}{R}$.

$P = \dfrac{1}{R}\left[\dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right)\right]^2$

Answer: $\dfrac{1}{R}\left[\dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right)\right]^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A straight, long wire carries a current $i$. Coplanar with the wire and perpendicular to it is a rod of length $l$. The ends of the rod slide on a pair of thick metallic rails laid parallel to the wire, and at one end the rails are connected by a resistor of resistance $R$. The distance of the wire from the centre of the rod is $x$. If the rod travels with a constant velocity $v$ in a direction parallel to the wire, calculate the power delivered by the external agent exerting the force on the rod.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by the long straight wire at a distance $r$ from it is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$

The rod of length $l$ is perpendicular to the wire and its centre is at a distance $x$ from the wire. Therefore, the ends of the rod are at distances $x - \dfrac{l}{2}$ and $x + \dfrac{l}{2}$ from the wire.

Consider a small element of the rod of length $dr$ at a distance $r$ from the wire. The motional emf induced in this element as it moves with velocity $v$ parallel to the wire is:

$dE = B v dr = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi r} dr$

The total induced emf $E$ in the rod is obtained by integrating $dE$ over the length of the rod:

$E = \int_{x - l/2}^{x + l/2} \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi r} dr$

$E = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} [\ln r]_{x - l/2}^{x + l/2}$

$E = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{x + l/2}{x - l/2}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right)$

Since the rod moves with a constant velocity, the mechanical power delivered by the external agent is equal to the electrical power dissipated as heat in the resistor $R$.

The power dissipated is:

$P = \dfrac{E^2}{R}$

Substituting the value of $E$, we get:

$P = \dfrac{1}{R}\left[\dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right)\right]^2$

Answer: $\dfrac{1}{R}\left[\dfrac{\mu_0 i v}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{2x + l}{2x - l}\right)\right]^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a square frame of wire having a total resistance $r$ is placed coplanarly with a long, straight wire. The straight wire carries a current $i$ given by $i = i_0 \sin \omega t$. Determine the flux of the magnetic field through the square frame.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by a long straight wire carrying current $i$ at a distance $x$ is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi x}$

Consider an elementary rectangular strip of the square frame at a distance $x$ from the wire, with width $dx$ and length $a$. The area of this strip is:

$dA = a \, dx$

The magnetic flux through this elementary strip is:

$d\phi = B \, dA = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi x} a \, dx$

The total magnetic flux through the square frame is obtained by integrating $d\phi$ from $x = b$ to $x = b + a$:

$\phi = \int_{b}^{b+a} \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi x} dx$

$\phi = \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi} [\ln x]_{b}^{b+a}$

$\phi = \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi} (\ln(b+a) - \ln b)$

$\phi = \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{b+a}{b}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi} \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a square frame of wire having a total resistance $r$ is placed coplanarly with a long, straight wire. The straight wire carries a current $i$ given by $i = i_0 \sin \omega t$. Determine the magnitude of the emf induced in the frame.
questions.solution:
Let us consider an elementary strip of width $dx$ in the square frame at a distance $x$ from the long straight wire.

The magnetic field at a distance $x$ due to the current-carrying straight wire is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi x}$

The area of this elementary strip is $dA = a \, dx$. The magnetic flux $d\Phi$ passing through this strip is:

$d\Phi = B \, dA = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi x} a \, dx$

The total magnetic flux $\Phi$ through the entire square frame is obtained by integrating $d\Phi$ from $x = b$ to $x = b + a$:

$\Phi = \int_{b}^{b+a} \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi x} dx = \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi} [\ln x]_{b}^{b+a} = \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{b+a}{b}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi} \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right)$

Given that the current is $i = i_0 \sin \omega t$, we substitute this into the flux equation:

$\Phi = \dfrac{\mu_0 a i_0 \sin \omega t}{2\pi} \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right)$

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the magnitude of the induced emf $e$ is:

$|e| = \left| \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \right| = \dfrac{\mu_0 a}{2\pi} \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right) \dfrac{d}{dt} (i_0 \sin \omega t)$

$|e| = \dfrac{\mu_0 a i_0 \omega \cos \omega t}{2\pi} \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a square frame of wire having a total resistance $r$ is placed coplanarly with a long, straight wire. The straight wire carries a current $i$ given by $i = i_0 \sin \omega t$. Calculate the heat developed in the frame during the time interval $0$ to $\dfrac{20\pi}{\omega}$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by the long straight wire at a distance $x$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi x}$.

Consider an elementary strip of width $dx$ in the square frame at a distance $x$ from the straight wire. The area of this strip is $dA = a \, dx$.

The magnetic flux through this elementary strip is $d\phi = B \, dA = \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi x} dx$.

The total magnetic flux through the square frame is obtained by integrating from $x = b$ to $x = b+a$:

$\phi = \int_{b}^{b+a} \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi x} dx = \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi} [\ln x]_{b}^{b+a} = \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi} \ln\left(\dfrac{b+a}{b}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi} \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right)$

Given the current is $i = i_0 \sin \omega t$, the flux as a function of time is:

$\phi(t) = \dfrac{\mu_0 a i_0}{2\pi} \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right) \sin \omega t$

According to Faraday's law of induction, the induced emf $e$ is:

$e = -\dfrac{d\phi}{dt} = -\dfrac{\mu_0 a i_0 \omega}{2\pi} \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right) \cos \omega t$

The instantaneous power dissipated as heat in the frame of resistance $r$ is $P = \dfrac{e^2}{r}$:

$P = \dfrac{\mu_0^2 a^2 i_0^2 \omega^2}{4\pi^2 r} \ln^2\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right) \cos^2 \omega t$

The total heat $H$ developed during the time interval $t = 0$ to $t = \dfrac{20\pi}{\omega}$ is:

$H = \int_{0}^{\frac{20\pi}{\omega}} P \, dt = \dfrac{\mu_0^2 a^2 i_0^2 \omega^2}{4\pi^2 r} \ln^2\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right) \int_{0}^{\frac{20\pi}{\omega}} \cos^2 \omega t \, dt$

Evaluating the integral:

$\int_{0}^{\frac{20\pi}{\omega}} \cos^2 \omega t \, dt = \int_{0}^{\frac{20\pi}{\omega}} \dfrac{1 + \cos 2\omega t}{2} \, dt = \left[ \dfrac{t}{2} + \dfrac{\sin 2\omega t}{4\omega} \right]_{0}^{\frac{20\pi}{\omega}} = \dfrac{10\pi}{\omega}$

Substituting this back into the expression for heat:

$H = \dfrac{\mu_0^2 a^2 i_0^2 \omega^2}{4\pi^2 r} \ln^2\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right) \left( \dfrac{10\pi}{\omega} \right)$

$H = \dfrac{5 \mu_0^2 a^2 i_0^2 \omega}{2\pi r} \ln^2\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rectangular metallic loop having length $l$ and width $b$ is situated coplanar with a long wire carrying a current $i$ (as shown in the figure). The loop travels perpendicular to the wire at a speed $v$ within the plane containing both the wire and the loop. Determine the emf induced in the loop at the instant the rear end of the loop is at a distance $a$ from the wire.
options: $\dfrac{\mu_0 i b v (2a + l)}{2\pi a (a + l)}$
$\dfrac{\mu_0 i l v b}{\pi a (a + l)}$
$\dfrac{\mu_0 i l v b}{2\pi a (a + l)}$
$\dfrac{\mu_0 i l v b}{4\pi a (a + l)}$
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by the long straight wire at a distance $x$ is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi x}$

The loop is moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to the wire. The motional emf induced in a wire of length $L$ moving in a perpendicular magnetic field is $\varepsilon = B L v$.

For the horizontal sides of the loop (length $l$), the velocity $\vec{v}$ is parallel to the length, so the induced emf is zero.

For the left vertical side (length $b$) which is at a distance $a$ from the wire, the induced emf is:

$\varepsilon_1 = B_1 b v = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi a} b v$

For the right vertical side (length $b$) which is at a distance $a+l$ from the wire, the induced emf is:

$\varepsilon_2 = B_2 b v = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi (a+l)} b v$

By Lenz's law or right-hand rule, these two emfs oppose each other in the loop. The net induced emf is the difference between them:

$\varepsilon_{net} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i b v}{2\pi} \left( \dfrac{1}{a} - \dfrac{1}{a+l} \right)$

$\varepsilon_{net} = \dfrac{\mu_0 i b v}{2\pi} \left( \dfrac{a + l - a}{a(a+l)} \right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 i l v b}{2\pi a (a+l)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular loop of radius $a$ is situated in a vertical plane within a uniform magnetic field $B$ directed perpendicular to the loop. A rod $OA$ of length $a$ and mass $m$ is pivoted at the loop's centre $O$, with its other end $A$ maintaining contact with the loop. While the rod and the loop possess negligible resistance, a wire connecting $O$ to a point $C$ on the loop has a resistance $R$. By applying a force at the midpoint of the rod $OA$ in a perpendicular direction, the rod is forced to rotate clockwise with a uniform angular velocity $\omega$. Determine the magnitude of this applied force when the rod is at an angle $\theta$ with the vertical.
questions.solution:
The motional emf $e$ induced in the rotating rod $OA$ is given by $e = \\dfrac{1}{2} B \\omega a^2$.

Since the rod and loop have negligible resistance, the total resistance of the circuit is $R$ (from the wire $OC$).

The induced current in the circuit is $I = \\dfrac{e}{R} = \\dfrac{B \\omega a^2}{2R}$.

The magnetic force on an element of the rod of length $dr$ at a distance $r$ from $O$ is $dF_m = I B dr$.

The magnetic torque about the pivot $O$ opposes the rotation:

$\\tau_m = \\int_0^a r dF_m = \\int_0^a I B r dr = I B \\left[ \\dfrac{r^2}{2} \\right]_0^a = \\dfrac{I B a^2}{2}$

Substituting the value of $I$:

$\\tau_m = \\left(\\dfrac{B \\omega a^2}{2R}\\right) \\dfrac{B a^2}{2} = \\dfrac{\\omega a^4 B^2}{4R}$

The torque due to gravity assists the clockwise rotation and acts at the centre of mass of the rod (distance $a/2$ from $O$):

$\\tau_g = mg \\left(\\dfrac{a}{2}\\right) \\sin\\theta$

Let the applied force at the midpoint of the rod be $F$. Its torque about $O$ is $\\tau_F = F \\left(\\dfrac{a}{2}\\right)$.

Since the rod rotates with a uniform angular velocity, the net torque must be zero. Balancing the torques:

$\\tau_F + \\tau_g = \\tau_m$

$F \\left(\\dfrac{a}{2}\\right) + mg \\left(\\dfrac{a}{2}\\right) \\sin\\theta = \\dfrac{\\omega a^4 B^2}{4R}$

Dividing the entire equation by $\\dfrac{a}{2}$:

$F + mg \\sin\\theta = \\dfrac{\\omega a^3 B^2}{2R}$

$F = \\dfrac{\\omega a^3 B^2}{2R} - mg \\sin\\theta$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, suppose the wire connecting $O$ and $C$ has zero resistance while the circular loop possesses a resistance $R$ uniformly distributed along its length. The rod $OA$ is made to rotate with a uniform angular speed $\omega$. Determine the current in the rod when $\angle AOC = 90^\circ$.
questions.solution:
The motional emf $e$ induced in the rotating rod $OA$ is given by $e = \\dfrac{1}{2} B \\omega a^2$.
