The circular loop has a total resistance $R$ uniformly distributed along its length.

When $\\angle AOC = 90^\\circ$, the points $A$ and $C$ divide the loop into two arcs: a minor arc of angle $90^\\circ$ and a major arc of angle $270^\\circ$.

The resistance of the minor arc is $R_1 = \\dfrac{90^\\circ}{360^\\circ} R = \\dfrac{R}{4}$.

The resistance of the major arc is $R_2 = \\dfrac{270^\\circ}{360^\\circ} R = \\dfrac{3R}{4}$.

Since the current from the rod at $A$ can flow to $C$ through either arc, these two arcs are in parallel between $A$ and $C$.

The equivalent resistance $R_{eq}$ of the circuit is:

$R_{eq} = \\dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \\dfrac{\\left(\\dfrac{R}{4}\\right)\\left(\\dfrac{3R}{4}\\right)}{R} = \\dfrac{3R}{16}$

The total current $I$ in the rod is:

$I = \\dfrac{e}{R_{eq}} = \\dfrac{\\dfrac{1}{2} B \\omega a^2}{\\dfrac{3R}{16}} = \\dfrac{8}{3} \\dfrac{\\omega a^2 B}{R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a conducting circular loop of radius $a$ is situated in a uniform, perpendicular magnetic field $B$. A thick metal rod $OA$ is pivoted at the centre $O$, and its other end touches the loop at $A$. A wire $OC$ with resistance $R$ connects the centre $O$ to a fixed point $C$ on the loop. A force is applied perpendicularly at the midpoint of the rod $OA$, causing the rod to rotate clockwise at a uniform angular velocity $\omega$. Determine the magnitude of the applied force.
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in the rotating rod $OA$ of length $a$ is given by:
$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{2} B \omega a^2$

Since the circular loop is conducting, the rod $OA$ and the wire $OC$ form a closed electrical circuit with resistance $R$. The induced current $I$ flowing through this circuit is:
$I = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{B \omega a^2}{2R}$

The magnetic force on a small element $dx$ of the rod at a distance $x$ from the center $O$ is $dF_m = I B dx$. The total magnetic torque $\tau_m$ opposing the rotation about $O$ is:
$\tau_m = \int_0^a x dF_m = \int_0^a I B x dx = \dfrac{1}{2} I B a^2$

The external force $F$ is applied perpendicularly at the midpoint of the rod (at a distance $\dfrac{a}{2}$ from $O$). The torque due to this applied force is:
$\tau_{app} = F \times \dfrac{a}{2}$

For the rod to rotate at a uniform angular velocity, the net torque must be zero. Therefore, the applied torque must balance the magnetic torque:
$\tau_{app} = \tau_m$

$F \dfrac{a}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} I B a^2$

$F = I B a$

Substituting the value of the induced current $I$ into the equation for $F$:
$F = \left( \dfrac{B \omega a^2}{2R} \right) B a = \dfrac{\omega a^3 B^2}{2R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular loop of radius $a$ is situated in a vertical plane within a uniform magnetic field $B$ directed perpendicular to the plane of the loop. A rod $OA$ of length $a$ and mass $m$ is pivoted at the loop's centre $O$, with its other end touching the loop at $A$. Between $O$ and a point $C$ on the loop, a battery of emf $\mathcal{E}$ and a variable resistance $R$ are connected. The connecting wires, the rod, and the loop have negligible resistance. Let $\theta$ denote the clockwise angle the rod makes with the horizontal position. During the interval $0 < \theta < \pi/4$, the rod is acted upon only by gravity, the magnetic field, and the pivot. If the resistance $R$ is continuously adjusted so that the rod rotates with a steady angular velocity $\omega$, determine the required value of $R$.
options: $\dfrac{\omega a B (\mathcal{E} - \omega a^2 B)}{2 m g \cos\theta}$
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in the rotating rod $OA$ is given by $e = \\dfrac{1}{2} B \\omega a^2$.

The net emf driving current through the circuit is $\\mathcal{E} - e$, assuming the battery assists the rotation against gravity.

The current in the circuit is $I = \\dfrac{\\mathcal{E} - \\dfrac{1}{2} B \\omega a^2}{R}$.

The magnetic force on an element $dr$ of the rod at distance $r$ from $O$ is $dF_m = I B dr$.

The magnetic torque about the pivot $O$ is $\\tau_m = \\int_0^a r (I B dr) = \\dfrac{I B a^2}{2}$.

The torque due to gravity about $O$ opposes the magnetic torque. Since the angle $\\theta$ is measured from the horizontal, the horizontal distance to the centre of mass is $\\dfrac{a}{2} \\cos\\theta$.

Thus, the gravitational torque is $\\tau_g = m g \\left(\\dfrac{a}{2}\\right) \\cos\\theta$.

For the rod to rotate with a constant angular velocity $\\omega$, the net torque must be zero, so $\\tau_m = \\tau_g$.

$\\dfrac{I B a^2}{2} = m g \\dfrac{a}{2} \\cos\\theta \\implies I B a = m g \\cos\\theta$

Substituting the expression for current $I$:

$\\left( \\dfrac{\\mathcal{E} - \\dfrac{1}{2} B \\omega a^2}{R} \\right) B a = m g \\cos\\theta$

Solving for $R$:

$R = \\dfrac{B a \\left(\\mathcal{E} - \\dfrac{1}{2} B \\omega a^2\\right)}{m g \\cos\\theta}$

$R = \\dfrac{a B (2\\mathcal{E} - \\omega a^2 B)}{2 m g \\cos\\theta}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a wire of mass $m$ and length $l$ that is free to slide on a pair of smooth vertical rails, as shown in the figure. A magnetic field $B$ is present in the region, directed perpendicular to the plane of the rails. A capacitor of capacitance $C$ connects the rails at their top end. Neglecting any electrical resistance, determine the acceleration of the wire.
questions.solution:
Let the velocity of the wire at any instant $t$ be $v$ downwards.

The motional emf induced in the wire is given by $e = Bvl$.

Since the electrical resistance of the circuit is negligible, the potential difference across the capacitor is equal to the induced emf, so $V = Bvl$.

The charge on the capacitor at this instant is $q = CV = CBvl$.

The current in the circuit is the rate of change of charge:

$i = \dfrac{dq}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}(CBvl) = CBl\dfrac{dv}{dt} = CBla$

where $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ is the acceleration of the wire.

The magnetic force acting on the current-carrying wire is:

$F_m = ilB = (CBla)lB = CB^2l^2a$

According to Lenz's law, this magnetic force opposes the downward motion of the wire, so it acts vertically upwards.

Applying Newton's second law for the downward motion of the wire:

$mg - F_m = ma$

$mg - CB^2l^2a = ma$

$mg = a(m + CB^2l^2)$

$a = \dfrac{mg}{m + CB^2l^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current in an ideal, long solenoid is changing at a steady rate of $0.01 \text{ A s}^{-1}$. The solenoid features $2000 \text{ turns/m}$ and a radius of $6.0 \text{ cm}$. Consider a circular loop of radius $1.0 \text{ cm}$ located inside the solenoid, with its axis matching the solenoid's axis. Determine the change in the magnetic flux through this loop over a time interval of $2.0 \text{ seconds}$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field inside a long solenoid is given by $B = \mu_0 n i$

The magnetic flux through the small circular loop is $\Phi = B A = \mu_0 n i (\pi r^2)$

The change in magnetic flux over a time interval $\Delta t$ is $\Delta \Phi = \mu_0 n (\pi r^2) \Delta i$

The change in current over $\Delta t = 2.0 \text{ s}$ is $\Delta i = \left(\dfrac{di}{dt}\right) \Delta t = 0.01 \times 2.0 = 0.02 \text{ A}$

Substituting the given values:

$\Delta \Phi = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 2000 \times \pi (0.01)^2 \times 0.02$

$\Delta \Phi = 16 \pi^2 \times 10^{-10}$

Using $\pi^2 \approx 9.87$, we get:

$\Delta \Phi \approx 157.9 \times 10^{-10} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-8} \text{ Wb}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform magnetic field $B$ exists in a cylindrical region, indicated by the dotted line in the figure. The magnetic field increases at a constant rate $\dfrac{dB}{dt}$. Consider a circle of radius $r$ coaxial with the cylindrical region. Determine the magnitude of the electric field $E$ at a point on the circumference of the circle.
questions.solution:
According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the magnitude of the induced electromotive force in a closed loop is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux through it:

$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = \left| \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \right|$

For a circle of radius $r$ coaxial with the cylindrical region, the magnetic flux enclosed is:

$\Phi = B \cdot \pi r^2$

The rate of change of magnetic flux is:

$\left| \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \right| = \pi r^2 \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

Due to the cylindrical symmetry of the magnetic field, the induced electric field $E$ is tangential to the circle and its magnitude is constant everywhere on the circumference. The line integral of the electric field around the circle is:

$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = E(2\pi r)$

Equating the two expressions, we get:

$E(2\pi r) = \pi r^2 \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

$E = \dfrac{r}{2} \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current within an ideal, long solenoid is varied at a constant rate of $0.01 \text{ A s}^{-1}$. The solenoid comprises $2000 \text{ turns/m}$ and possesses a radius of $6.0 \text{ cm}$. Consider a circle of radius $1.0 \text{ cm}$ placed inside the solenoid such that its axis aligns with the axis of the solenoid. Determine the electric field induced at a point on the circumference of this circle.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field inside an ideal long solenoid is given by:

$B = \mu_0 n i$

The rate of change of the magnetic field is:

$\dfrac{dB}{dt} = \mu_0 n \dfrac{di}{dt}$

According to Faraday's law of induction, the induced electromotive force along a circular path of radius $r$ inside the solenoid is:

$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = \left| \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \right|$

Since the electric field $E$ is uniform along the circumference due to symmetry, and the magnetic flux is $\Phi = B \pi r^2$ (as the circle is completely inside the solenoid, $r < R$), we get:

$E (2\pi r) = \pi r^2 \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

$E = \dfrac{r}{2} \dfrac{dB}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{2} r \mu_0 n \dfrac{di}{dt}$

Substituting the given values $r = 1.0 \text{ cm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$, $n = 2000 \text{ m}^{-1}$, and $\dfrac{di}{dt} = 0.01 \text{ A s}^{-1}$:

$E = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.01 \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 2000 \times 0.01$

$E = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 10^{-2} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 20$

$E = 40\pi \times 10^{-9} \text{ V m}^{-1}$

$E = 4\pi \times 10^{-8} \text{ V m}^{-1}$

Using $\pi \approx 3.1416$:

$E \approx 4 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-8} \text{ V m}^{-1} = 1.256 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V m}^{-1}$

Rounding to three significant figures, we obtain $1.26 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V m}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current flowing through an ideal, long solenoid varies at a uniform rate of $0.01 \text{ A s}^{-1}$. The solenoid has $2000 \text{ turns/m}$ and a radius of $6.0 \text{ cm}$. Determine the electric field induced at a point situated outside the solenoid, at a perpendicular distance of $8.0 \text{ cm}$ from its axis.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field inside an ideal long solenoid is given by $B = \mu_0 n i$.

The magnetic flux through a circular loop of radius $r$ ($r > R$) coaxial with the solenoid is confined to the area of the solenoid:

$\Phi = B \times \pi R^2 = \mu_0 n i \pi R^2$

By Faraday's law of induction, the magnitude of the induced electromotive force is:

$\epsilon = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = \mu_0 n \pi R^2 \dfrac{di}{dt}$

The induced electric field $E$ along the circular loop of radius $r$ is related to the induced emf by:

$\epsilon = \oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = E(2\pi r)$

Equating the two expressions for $\epsilon$:

$E(2\pi r) = \mu_0 n \pi R^2 \dfrac{di}{dt}$

$E = \dfrac{\mu_0 n R^2}{2r} \dfrac{di}{dt}$

Substituting the given values $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$, $n = 2000 \text{ m}^{-1}$, $R = 0.06 \text{ m}$, $r = 0.08 \text{ m}$, and $\dfrac{di}{dt} = 0.01 \text{ A s}^{-1}$:

$E = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2000 \times (0.06)^2}{2 \times 0.08} \times 0.01$

$E = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2000 \times 0.0036 \times 0.01}{0.16}$

$E = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 0.072}{0.16}$

$E = 1.8\pi \times 10^{-7}$

$E = 1.8 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-7} = 5.65 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V m}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the current in an inductor changes from $2.5 \text{ A}$ in one direction to the same value in the opposite direction over a period of $0.1 \text{ s}$, an average emf of $20 \text{ V}$ is induced. Determine the self-inductance of the inductor.
questions.solution:
The average induced emf in an inductor is given by the formula:

$|E| = L \left| \dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta t} \right|$

Given initial current $I_1 = 2.5 \text{ A}$ and final current $I_2 = -2.5 \text{ A}$, the change in current is:

$\Delta I = I_2 - I_1 = -2.5 - 2.5 = -5.0 \text{ A}$

The time interval is $\Delta t = 0.1 \text{ s}$ and the average induced emf is $|E| = 20 \text{ V}$. Substituting these values into the formula:

$20 = L \left| \dfrac{-5.0}{0.1} \right|$

$20 = L \times 50$

$L = \dfrac{20}{50} = 0.4 \text{ H}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When an electric current of $4 \text{ A}$ flows in a $200$-turn coil, a magnetic flux of $8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ weber}$ is linked with each turn. Determine the self-inductance of the coil.
questions.solution:
The total magnetic flux linked with the coil is given by $N \Phi = L I$, where $N$ is the number of turns, $\Phi$ is the flux linked with each turn, $L$ is the self-inductance, and $I$ is the current.

Given:
$N = 200$
$I = 4 \text{ A}$
$\Phi = 8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Wb}$

Substituting the values into the formula:
$L = \dfrac{N \Phi}{I}$

$L = \dfrac{200 \times 8 \times 10^{-4}}{4}$

$L = \dfrac{1600 \times 10^{-4}}{4}$

$L = 400 \times 10^{-4} \text{ H}$

$L = 0.04 \text{ H}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solenoid of $240$ turns has a length of $12 \text{ cm}$ and a radius of $2 \text{ cm}$. If the current in the solenoid changes at a rate of $0.8 \text{ A s}^{-1}$, determine the induced emf.
questions.solution:
The self-inductance of a solenoid is given by:

$L = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l} = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 \pi r^2}{l}$

Substituting the given values:

$L = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times (240)^2 \times \pi \times (0.02)^2}{0.12}$

$L = \dfrac{16\pi^2 \times 10^{-11} \times 57600}{0.12} = 768 \pi^2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ H}$

The magnitude of the induced emf is:

$e = L \dfrac{di}{dt}$

$e = (768 \pi^2 \times 10^{-7}) \times 0.8$

$e = 614.4 \pi^2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V}$

Using $\pi^2 \approx 9.87$:

$e \approx 614.4 \times 9.87 \times 10^{-7} \approx 6.06 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$

This is approximately $6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the value of $t/\tau$ at which the current in an $LR$ circuit builds up to $99\%$ of the steady-state value.
questions.solution:
The growth of current in an $LR$ circuit is given by the equation:

$I = I_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

where $I_0$ is the steady-state current and $\tau$ is the time constant.

Given that the current reaches $99\%$ of its steady-state value, we have $I = 0.99 I_0$.

Substituting this into the equation:

$0.99 I_0 = I_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

$0.99 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau}$

$e^{-t/\tau} = 0.01$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$-\dfrac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.01)$

$-\dfrac{t}{\tau} = \ln(10^{-2})$

$-\dfrac{t}{\tau} = -2 \ln(10)$

Using the value $\ln(10) \approx 2.303$:

$\dfrac{t}{\tau} = 2 \times 2.303 = 4.606 \approx 4.6$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the value of $t/\tau$ at which the current in an $LR$ circuit builds up to $90\%$ of the steady-state value.
questions.solution:
The growth of current in an $LR$ circuit is given by the equation:

$I = I_{0}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

where $I_{0}$ is the steady-state current and $\tau$ is the time constant.

Given that the current builds up to $90\%$ of its steady-state value, we have $I = 0.9 I_{0}$. Substituting this into the equation gives:

$0.9 I_{0} = I_{0}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

$0.9 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau}$

$e^{-t/\tau} = 0.1$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$-\dfrac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.1)$

$\dfrac{t}{\tau} = \ln(10)$

$\dfrac{t}{\tau} \approx 2.3$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the value of $t/\tau$ at which the current in an $LR$ circuit builds up to $99.9\%$ of the steady-state value.
questions.solution:
The growth of current in an $LR$ circuit is given by the equation:

$I = I_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

Given that the current builds up to $99.9\%$ of its steady-state value, we have $I = 0.999 I_0$.

Substituting this into the equation:

$0.999 I_0 = I_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

$0.999 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau}$

$e^{-t/\tau} = 1 - 0.999 = 0.001$

$e^{-t/\tau} = 10^{-3}$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$-\dfrac{t}{\tau} = \ln(10^{-3})$

$-\dfrac{t}{\tau} = -3 \ln(10)$

Using the value $\ln(10) \approx 2.303$:

$\dfrac{t}{\tau} = 3 \times 2.303 = 6.909 \approx 6.9$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When connected across an ideal battery of emf $4.0 \text{ V}$, an inductor-coil carries a steady-state current of $2.0 \text{ A}$. Determine the time constant of the circuit if its inductance is $1.0 \text{ H}$.
questions.solution:
In steady state, the inductor acts as a short circuit and the current is limited only by the resistance of the coil.

The resistance of the coil is given by:

$R = \dfrac{E}{I_{0}}$

Substituting the given values:

$R = \dfrac{4.0}{2.0} = 2.0 \ \Omega$

The time constant of an $L-R$ circuit is given by:

$\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$

Substituting the values of $L$ and $R$:

$\tau = \dfrac{1.0}{2.0} = 0.5 \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil with an inductance of $2.0 \text{ H}$ and a resistance of $20 \text{ }\Omega$ is connected to a battery having an emf of $4.0 \text{ V}$. Determine the current at the instant $0.20 \text{ s}$ after the connection is made.
questions.solution:
The growth of current in an $L-R$ circuit is given by the equation:

$I = I_{0} (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

The steady-state current $I_{0}$ is:

$I_{0} = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{4.0}{20} = 0.20 \text{ A}$

The time constant $\tau$ is:

$\tau = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{2.0}{20} = 0.10 \text{ s}$

Substituting the values at $t = 0.20 \text{ s}$:

$I = 0.20 (1 - e^{-0.20/0.10})$

$I = 0.20 (1 - e^{-2})$

Using $e^{-2} \approx 0.135$:

$I = 0.20 (1 - 0.135) = 0.20 \times 0.865 = 0.173 \text{ A}$

Rounding to two significant figures, the current is $0.17 \text{ A}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil with an inductance of $2.0 \text{ H}$ and a resistance of $20 \text{ }\Omega$ is connected to a battery having an emf of $4.0 \text{ V}$. Determine the magnetic field energy at the instant $0.20 \text{ s}$ after the connection is made.
questions.solution:
The growth of current in an $LR$ circuit is given by

$i = i_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

where the steady state current $i_0 = \dfrac{E}{R}$ and the time constant $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$.

Substituting the given values:

$i_0 = \dfrac{4.0}{20} = 0.2 \text{ A}$

$\tau = \dfrac{2.0}{20} = 0.1 \text{ s}$

At $t = 0.20 \text{ s}$, the current is:

$i = 0.2 (1 - e^{-0.20/0.1}) = 0.2 (1 - e^{-2})$

Using $e^{-2} \approx 0.135$:

$i \approx 0.2 (1 - 0.135) = 0.2 \times 0.865 = 0.173 \text{ A}$

The magnetic field energy stored in the inductor is:

$U = \dfrac{1}{2} L i^2$

$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 2.0 \times (0.173)^2 \approx 0.0299 \text{ J} \approx 0.03 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil with a resistance of $40 \text{ }\Omega$ is connected across a $4.0 \text{ V}$ battery. If the current in the coil is $63 \text{ mA}$ exactly $0.10 \text{ s}$ after making the connection, determine the inductance of the coil.
questions.solution:
The growth of current in an LR circuit is given by the equation:

$I = I_{0}(1 - e^{-Rt/L})$

The steady state maximum current $I_{0}$ is:

$I_{0} = \dfrac{V}{R} = \dfrac{4.0}{40} = 0.1 \text{ A} = 100 \text{ mA}$

Given that the current $I = 63 \text{ mA}$ at time $t = 0.10 \text{ s}$, substituting these values into the equation gives:

$63 = 100(1 - e^{-40 \times 0.10 / L})$

$0.63 = 1 - e^{-4.0/L}$

$e^{-4.0/L} = 1 - 0.63 = 0.37$

Since $e^{-1} \approx 0.37$, equating the exponents gives:

$\dfrac{4.0}{L} = 1$

$L = 4.0 \text{ H}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An $LR$ circuit has a time constant of $40 \text{ ms}$. The circuit is connected at $t = 0$ and the steady-state current is found to be $2.0 \text{ A}$. Determine the current at $t = 20 \text{ ms}$.
questions.solution:
The growth of current in an $LR$ circuit is given by the equation:

$I(t) = I_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

Given:
Time constant, $\tau = 40 \text{ ms}$
Steady-state current, $I_0 = 2.0 \text{ A}$
Time, $t = 20 \text{ ms}$

Substituting the values into the equation:

$I = 2.0 (1 - e^{-20/40})$

$I = 2.0 (1 - e^{-0.5})$

Using the value $e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$:

$I = 2.0 (1 - 0.6065)$

$I = 2.0 \times 0.3935$

$I = 0.787 \text{ A} \approx 0.79 \text{ A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An $LR$ circuit has a time constant of $40 \text{ ms}$. The circuit is connected at $t = 0$ and the steady-state current is found to be $2.0 \text{ A}$. Determine the current at $t = 10 \text{ ms}$.
questions.solution:
The growth of current in an $LR$ circuit is given by the equation:

$I(t) = I_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

Given $I_0 = 2.0 \text{ A}$, $\tau = 40 \text{ ms}$, and $t = 10 \text{ ms}$.

Substituting the values into the equation:

$I(10 \text{ ms}) = 2.0 (1 - e^{-10/40})$

$I(10 \text{ ms}) = 2.0 (1 - e^{-0.25})$

Using the approximate value $e^{-0.25} \approx 0.7788$:

$I(10 \text{ ms}) = 2.0 (1 - 0.7788)$

$I(10 \text{ ms}) = 2.0 \times 0.2212 = 0.4424 \text{ A}$

Rounding to two decimal places, the current is $0.44 \text{ A}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductor with an inductance of $5.0 \text{ H}$ and negligible resistance is connected in series with a $100 \text{ }\Omega$ resistor and a battery having an emf of $2.0 \text{ V}$. Determine the potential difference across the resistor $20 \text{ ms}$ after switching on the circuit.
questions.solution:
The current in an L-R circuit during growth is given by $I = I_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, where $I_0 = \dfrac{E}{R}$ is the steady state current and $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$ is the time constant.

The potential difference across the resistor at time $t$ is:

$V_R = I R = E(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

Given values:

$E = 2.0 \text{ V}$

$L = 5.0 \text{ H}$

$R = 100 \text{ }\Omega$

$t = 20 \text{ ms} = 0.02 \text{ s}$

The time constant is:

$\tau = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{5.0}{100} = 0.05 \text{ s}$

Substituting these values into the equation for $V_R$:

$V_R = 2.0 \times (1 - e^{-0.02/0.05})$

$V_R = 2.0 \times (1 - e^{-0.4})$

Using the value $e^{-0.4} \approx 0.67$:

$V_R = 2.0 \times (1 - 0.67)$

$V_R = 2.0 \times 0.33 = 0.66 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An $LR$ circuit has a time constant of $40 \text{ ms}$. The circuit is connected at $t = 0$ and the steady-state current is found to be $2.0 \text{ A}$. Determine the current at $t = 1 \text{ s}$.
questions.solution:
The growth of current in an $LR$ circuit is given by the equation:

$I = I_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

Given the steady-state current $I_0 = 2.0 \text{ A}$ and time constant $\tau = 40 \text{ ms} = 0.04 \text{ s}$.

Substituting $t = 1 \text{ s}$ into the equation:

$I = 2.0 (1 - e^{-1/0.04})$

$I = 2.0 (1 - e^{-25})$

Since $e^{-25}$ is negligibly small ($e^{-25} \approx 0$), the current is:

$I \approx 2.0 (1 - 0) = 2.0 \text{ A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An $LR$ circuit has a time constant of $40 \text{ ms}$. The circuit is connected at $t = 0$ and the steady-state current is found to be $2.0 \text{ A}$. Determine the current at $t = 100 \text{ ms}$.
questions.solution:
The equation for the growth of current in an $LR$ circuit is given by

$I(t) = I_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

Substituting the given values $I_0 = 2.0 \text{ A}$, $t = 100 \text{ ms}$, and $\tau = 40 \text{ ms}$:

$I = 2.0 (1 - e^{-100/40})$

$I = 2.0 (1 - e^{-2.5})$

Using the value $e^{-2.5} \approx 0.082$:

$I = 2.0 (1 - 0.082)$

$I = 2.0 \times 0.918 = 1.836 \text{ A}$

Rounding to appropriate significant figures, $I \approx 1.8 \text{ A}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An $LR$ circuit with $L = 1.0 \text{ H}$ and $R = 20 \text{ }\Omega$ is connected across a $2.0 \text{ V}$ emf at $t = 0$. Determine the rate of change of current $di/dt$ at $t = 100 \text{ ms}$.
questions.solution:
The equation for the growth of current in an $LR$ circuit is given by

$i = i_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

where $i_0 = \dfrac{V}{R}$ is the steady state current and $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$ is the time constant.

The rate of change of current is

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt} \left[ \dfrac{V}{R} (1 - e^{-Rt/L}) \right] = \dfrac{V}{L} e^{-Rt/L}$

Given values are $V = 2.0 \text{ V}$, $L = 1.0 \text{ H}$, $R = 20 \text{ } \Omega$, and $t = 100 \text{ ms} = 0.1 \text{ s}$.

Substituting these values, we get

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{2.0}{1.0} e^{-20 \times 0.1 / 1.0}$

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = 2.0 e^{-2}$

Using $e \approx 2.718$, we have $e^2 \approx 7.389$.

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{2.0}{7.389} \approx 0.27 \text{ A s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, an $LR$ circuit having $L = 1.0 \text{ H}$ and $R = 20 \text{ }\Omega$ is connected across an emf of $2.0 \text{ V}$. Find the rate of change of current $di/dt$ at $t = 200 \text{ ms}$.
questions.solution:
The current in an $LR$ circuit at time $t$ is given by $i = \dfrac{E}{R}(1 - e^{-Rt/L})$

Differentiating with respect to $t$, the rate of change of current is:

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{E}{L} e^{-Rt/L}$

Given $E = 2.0 \text{ V}$, $L = 1.0 \text{ H}$, $R = 20 \text{ }\Omega$, and $t = 200 \text{ ms} = 0.2 \text{ s}$

Substituting the values:

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{2.0}{1.0} e^{-20 \times 0.2 / 1.0}$

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = 2 e^{-4}$

Using $e^{-4} \approx 0.0183$:

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = 2 \times 0.0183 = 0.0366 \text{ A s}^{-1} \approx 0.037 \text{ A s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, an $LR$ circuit with $L = 1.0 	ext{ H}$ and $R = 20 	ext{ }\Omega$ is connected across an emf of $2.0 	ext{ V}$. What is the value of the self-induced emf in the circuit at $t = 100 	ext{ ms}$?
questions.solution:
The time constant of the $LR$ circuit is given by:

$\tau = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{1.0}{20} = 0.05 \text{ s}$

The current in the circuit at any time $t$ is:

$I = \dfrac{E}{R} (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

The magnitude of the self-induced emf is:

$|e| = L \dfrac{dI}{dt} = L \dfrac{d}{dt} \left[ \dfrac{E}{R} (1 - e^{-t/\tau}) \right]$

$|e| = L \dfrac{E}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau} \right)$

Substituting $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$ gives:

$|e| = E e^{-t/\tau}$

Substituting the given values $E = 2.0 \text{ V}$ and $t = 100 \text{ ms} = 0.1 \text{ s}$:

$|e| = 2.0 \times e^{-0.1 / 0.05} = 2.0 \times e^{-2}$

Using the value $e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$:

$|e| = 2.0 \times 0.1353 = 0.2706 \text{ V} \approx 0.27 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An $LR$ circuit features $L = 1.0 \text{ H}$ and $R = 20 \text{ }\Omega$. At $t = 0$, it is connected across a $2.0 \text{ V}$ emf. Calculate the rate of change of current $di/dt$ at $t = 1.0 \text{ s}$.
questions.solution:
The current in an $LR$ circuit during growth is given by:

$i = \dfrac{E}{R} (1 - e^{-Rt/L})$

Differentiating with respect to time $t$, we get the rate of change of current:

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{E}{L} e^{-Rt/L}$

Substituting the given values $E = 2.0 \text{ V}$, $L = 1.0 \text{ H}$, $R = 20 \text{ }\Omega$, and $t = 1.0 \text{ s}$:

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{2.0}{1.0} e^{-20 \times 1.0 / 1.0}$

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = 2.0 \times e^{-20}$

Using the value $e^{-20} \approx 2.06 \times 10^{-9}$:

$\dfrac{di}{dt} \approx 2.0 \times 2.06 \times 10^{-9} = 4.12 \times 10^{-9} \text{ A s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, an $LR$ circuit with $L = 1.0 	ext{ H}$ and $R = 20 	ext{ }\Omega$ is connected across an emf of $2.0 	ext{ V}$. What is the value of the self-induced emf in the circuit at $t = 1.0 	ext{ s}$?
questions.solution:
The current in an $LR$ circuit at time $t$ is given by $I = \dfrac{E}{R}(1 - e^{-Rt/L})$.

The magnitude of the self-induced emf is $e = L\dfrac{dI}{dt}$.

Differentiating the expression for current with respect to time, we get:

$\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \dfrac{E}{R} \times \dfrac{R}{L} e^{-Rt/L} = \dfrac{E}{L} e^{-Rt/L}$

Thus, the self-induced emf is:

$e = L \times \dfrac{E}{L} e^{-Rt/L} = E e^{-Rt/L}$

Substituting the given values $E = 2.0 \text{ V}$, $R = 20 \text{ }\Omega$, $L = 1.0 \text{ H}$, and $t = 1.0 \text{ s}$:

$e = 2.0 \times e^{-20 \times 1.0 / 1.0} = 2.0 \times e^{-20}$

To evaluate $e^{-20}$, taking the base $10$ logarithm gives:

$\log_{10}(e^{-20}) = -20 \log_{10}(e) = -20 \times 0.4343 = -8.686$

This can be written as $-9 + 0.314$. Since $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.301$ and $\log_{10}(2.1) \approx 0.322$, we have $10^{0.314} \approx 2.06$.

Therefore, $e^{-20} \approx 2.06 \times 10^{-9}$.

The self-induced emf is:

$e \approx 2.0 \times 2.06 \times 10^{-9} = 4.12 \times 10^{-9} \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, an $LR$ circuit with $L = 1.0 	ext{ H}$ and $R = 20 	ext{ }\Omega$ is connected across an emf of $2.0 	ext{ V}$. What is the value of the self-induced emf in the circuit at $t = 200 	ext{ ms}$?
questions.solution:
The current in an $LR$ circuit during growth is given by $I = I_{0}(1 - e^{-Rt/L})$.

The magnitude of the self-induced emf is given by $e = L\dfrac{dI}{dt}$.

Differentiating the current expression with respect to time, we get $e = L \left( I_{0} \dfrac{R}{L} e^{-Rt/L} \right) = E e^{-Rt/L}$, where $E$ is the applied emf.

Given $E = 2.0 \text{ V}$, $R = 20 \text{ }\Omega$, $L = 1.0 \text{ H}$, and $t = 200 \text{ ms} = 0.2 \text{ s}$.

Substituting the values, we get $e = 2.0 \times e^{-20 \times 0.2 / 1.0} = 2.0 \times e^{-4}$.

Using the value $e^{-4} \approx 0.0183$, the induced emf is $e = 2.0 \times 0.0183 = 0.0366 \text{ V} \approx 0.037 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductor-coil with an inductance of $20 \text{ mH}$ and a resistance of $10 \text{ }\Omega$ is connected to an ideal battery having an emf of $5.0 \text{ V}$. Determine the rate of change of the induced emf at $t = 0$.
questions.solution:
The current in an $L-R$ circuit at time $t$ is given by:

$i = \dfrac{E}{R} (1 - e^{-Rt/L})$

The induced emf $e$ across the inductor is:

$e = -L \dfrac{di}{dt}$

Differentiating $i$ with respect to $t$:

$\dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{E}{L} e^{-Rt/L}$

Thus, the induced emf is:

$e = -L \left( \dfrac{E}{L} e^{-Rt/L} \right) = -E e^{-Rt/L}$

The rate of change of the induced emf is:

$\dfrac{de}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt} (-E e^{-Rt/L}) = -E \left( -\dfrac{R}{L} \right) e^{-Rt/L} = \dfrac{ER}{L} e^{-Rt/L}$

At $t = 0$, the magnitude of the rate of change of the induced emf is:

$\left| \dfrac{de}{dt} \right| = \dfrac{ER}{L}$

Substituting the given values $E = 5.0 \text{ V}$, $R = 10 \text{ }\Omega$, and $L = 20 \text{ mH} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H}$:

$\left| \dfrac{de}{dt} \right| = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{50}{0.02} = 2500 \text{ V s}^{-1} = 2.5 \times 10^3 \text{ V s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An $LR$ circuit includes an inductor of $500 \text{ mH}$, a resistor of $25.0 \text{ }\Omega$, and a $5.00 \text{ V}$ emf connected in series. Determine the potential difference across the resistor at $t = 20.0 \text{ ms}$.
questions.solution:
The time constant of the $LR$ circuit is given by $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$.

Substituting the given values:

$\tau = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{-3}}{25.0} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20.0 \text{ ms}$

The current in the circuit at time $t$ is given by:

$I = \dfrac{E}{R} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$

The potential difference across the resistor is:

$V_R = IR = E \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$

At $t = 20.0 \text{ ms}$, we have $t = \tau$.

$V_R = 5.00 \left(1 - e^{-1}\right)$

$V_R = 5.00 \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2.718}\right)$

$V_R \approx 5.00 \times (1 - 0.368) = 5.00 \times 0.632 = 3.16 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductor-coil with an inductance of $20 \text{ mH}$ and a resistance of $10 \text{ }\Omega$ is connected to an ideal battery having an emf of $5.0 \text{ V}$. Determine the rate of change of the induced emf at $t = 1.0 \text{ s}$.
questions.solution:
The current in an LR circuit as a function of time is given by:

$i = \dfrac{E_0}{R} (1 - e^{-Rt/L})$

The induced emf in the inductor is:

$e = L \dfrac{di}{dt} = E_0 e^{-Rt/L}$

The rate of change of the induced emf is:

$\dfrac{de}{dt} = -E_0 \dfrac{R}{L} e^{-Rt/L}$

Given values are $E_0 = 5.0 \text{ V}$, $R = 10 \text{ }\Omega$, and $L = 20 \text{ mH} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H}$.

The time constant is $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{10} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$.

At $t = 1.0 \text{ s}$, the exponent is $-\dfrac{Rt}{L} = -\dfrac{1.0}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = -500$.

Substituting these values into the rate of change equation:

$\dfrac{de}{dt} = -5.0 \times 500 \times e^{-500}$

Since $e^{-500} \approx 0$, the rate of change of the induced emf is $0.00 \text{ V s}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductor-coil with an inductance of $20 \text{ mH}$ and a resistance of $10 \text{ }\Omega$ is connected to an ideal battery having an emf of $5.0 \text{ V}$. Determine the rate of change of the induced emf at $t = 10 \text{ ms}$.
questions.solution:
The current in an $L-R$ circuit during growth is given by:

$i = \dfrac{E}{R}(1 - e^{-Rt/L})$

The induced emf in the inductor is:

$\varepsilon = L\dfrac{di}{dt} = L \left( \dfrac{E}{R} \times \dfrac{R}{L} e^{-Rt/L} \right) = E e^{-Rt/L}$

The rate of change of the induced emf is:

$\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = -E \left( \dfrac{R}{L} \right) e^{-Rt/L}$

The magnitude of the rate of change of the induced emf is:

$\left| \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} \right| = \dfrac{ER}{L} e^{-Rt/L}$

Substituting the given values $E = 5.0 \text{ V}$, $R = 10 \Omega$, $L = 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H}$, and $t = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$:

$\dfrac{R}{L} = \dfrac{10}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = 500 \text{ s}^{-1}$

$\dfrac{Rt}{L} = 500 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} = 5$

$\left| \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} \right| = 5.0 \times 500 \times e^{-5} = 2500 \times e^{-5}$

Using the approximate value $e^{-5} \approx 0.00674$:

$\left| \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} \right| \approx 2500 \times 0.00674 \approx 16.85 \text{ V s}^{-1}$

Rounding to the nearest integer given in the options, we get $17 \text{ V s}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An $LR$ circuit includes an inductor of $500 \text{ mH}$, a resistor of $25.0 \text{ }\Omega$, and a $5.00 \text{ V}$ emf connected in series. Determine the potential difference across the resistor at $t = 100 \text{ ms}$.
questions.solution:
The time constant of the $LR$ circuit is given by $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$.

Substituting the given values:
$\tau = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{-3}}{25.0} = 0.02 \text{ s}$

The current in the circuit at time $t$ is:
$I(t) = I_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau}) = \dfrac{V_0}{R} (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

The potential difference across the resistor is:
$V_R = I(t) R = V_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

At $t = 100 \text{ ms} = 0.1 \text{ s}$:
$V_R = 5.00 (1 - e^{-0.1/0.02}) = 5.00 (1 - e^{-5})$

Using $e^{-5} \approx 0.0067$:
$V_R = 5.00 (1 - 0.0067) = 5.00 \times 0.9933 = 4.9665 \text{ V} \approx 4.97 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An $LR$ circuit includes an inductor of $500 \text{ mH}$, a resistor of $25.0 \text{ }\Omega$, and a $5.00 \text{ V}$ emf connected in series. Determine the potential difference across the resistor at $t = 1.00 \text{ s}$.
questions.solution:
The time constant of the $LR$ circuit is given by $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$.

Substituting the given values, $\tau = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{-3}}{25.0} = 0.02 \text{ s}$.

The current in the circuit as a function of time is $I(t) = \dfrac{E}{R} (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.

At $t = 1.00 \text{ s}$, the ratio $\dfrac{t}{\tau} = \dfrac{1.00}{0.02} = 50$.

Since $e^{-50} \approx 0$, the current has reached its steady-state value $I = \dfrac{E}{R}$.

The potential difference across the resistor is $V_R = I R = \left(\dfrac{E}{R}\right) R = E = 5.00 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductor-coil with a resistance of $10\ \Omega$ and an inductance of $120\ \text{mH}$ is connected to a battery having an emf of $6\ \text{V}$ and an internal resistance of $2\ \Omega$. Determine the charge that flows through the inductor-coil in $10\ \text{ms}$ after the connections are made.
questions.solution:
Total resistance of the circuit, $R = R_L + r = 10 + 2 = 12\ \Omega$

Time constant of the circuit, $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{120 \times 10^{-3}}{12} = 10\ \text{ms}$

Steady state current, $I_0 = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{6}{12} = 0.5\ \text{A}$

The current at any time $t$ is given by:

$i = I_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

The charge flown through the inductor in time $t$ is:

$q = \int_0^t i dt = \int_0^t I_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau}) dt$

$q = I_0 \left[ t + \tau e^{-t/\tau} \right]_0^t = I_0 \left( t - \tau(1 - e^{-t/\tau}) \right)$

Substituting $t = 10\ \text{ms} = \tau$:

$q = I_0 (\tau - \tau(1 - e^{-1})) = I_0 \tau e^{-1}$

$q = 0.5 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{1}{e}$

Using $e \approx 2.718$:

$q = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.718} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{C} = 1.84\ \text{mC}$

The closest value is $1.8\ \text{mC}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductor-coil with a resistance of $10\ \Omega$ and an inductance of $120\ \text{mH}$ is connected to a battery having an emf of $6\ \text{V}$ and an internal resistance of $2\ \Omega$. Determine the charge that flows through the inductor-coil in $20\ \text{ms}$ after the connections are made.
questions.solution:
The total resistance of the circuit is $R = 10 + 2 = 12\ \Omega$.

The time constant of the circuit is $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{120 \times 10^{-3}}{12} = 0.01\ \text{s}$.

The steady-state current is $i_0 = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{6}{12} = 0.5\ \text{A}$.

The current at any time $t$ is given by:

$i(t) = i_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

The charge $q$ that flows in time $t$ is the integral of current with respect to time:

$q = \int_{0}^{t} i(t) dt = \int_{0}^{t} i_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau}) dt$

$q = i_0 \left[ t + \tau e^{-t/\tau} \right]_{0}^{t} = i_0 \left( t - \tau + \tau e^{-t/\tau} \right)$

Substituting $t = 20\ \text{ms} = 0.02\ \text{s}$, $\tau = 0.01\ \text{s}$, and $i_0 = 0.5\ \text{A}$:

$q = 0.5 \left( 0.02 - 0.01 + 0.01 e^{-0.02/0.01} \right)$

$q = 0.5 \left( 0.01 + 0.01 e^{-2} \right) = 0.005(1 + e^{-2})\ \text{C}$

Using $e^{-2} \approx 0.135$:

$q \approx 0.005(1 + 0.135) = 0.005675\ \text{C} = 5.675\ \text{mC}$

Rounding to one decimal place, the charge is approximately $5.7\ \text{mC}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductor-coil with a resistance of $10\ \Omega$ and an inductance of $120\ \text{mH}$ is connected to a battery having an emf of $6\ \text{V}$ and an internal resistance of $2\ \Omega$. Determine the charge that flows through the inductor-coil in $100\ \text{ms}$ after the connections are made.
questions.solution:
Total resistance of the circuit, $R = 10 + 2 = 12\ \Omega$

Steady state current, $I_0 = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{6}{12} = 0.5\ \text{A}$

Time constant, $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{120 \times 10^{-3}}{12} = 0.01\ \text{s}$

The current in the circuit at any time $t$ is given by $i = I_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$

The charge flowing through the inductor in time $t$ is:

$q = \int_{0}^{t} i dt = \int_{0}^{t} I_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau}) dt$

$q = I_0 \left[ t + \tau e^{-t/\tau} \right]_{0}^{t} = I_0 \left( t - \tau(1 - e^{-t/\tau}) \right)$

Substituting $t = 100\ \text{ms} = 0.1\ \text{s}$, $\tau = 0.01\ \text{s}$, and $I_0 = 0.5\ \text{A}$:

$q = 0.5 \left( 0.1 - 0.01(1 - e^{-10}) \right)$

Since $e^{-10} \approx 0$, we get:

$q \approx 0.5 (0.1 - 0.01) = 0.5 \times 0.09 = 0.045\ \text{C} = 45\ \text{mC}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductor-coil with an inductance of $17 \text{ mH}$ is fabricated using a copper wire that has a length of $100 \text{ m}$ and a cross-sectional area of $1 \text{ mm}^2$. Determine the time constant of the circuit when this inductor is connected across an ideal battery. The resistivity of copper is $1.7 \times 10^{-8} \text{ }\Omega\text{m}$.
questions.solution:
Resistance of the copper wire is given by $R = \rho \dfrac{l}{A}$

Substituting the given values:

$R = \dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-8} \times 100}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.7 \text{ }\Omega$

The time constant of the LR circuit is given by $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$

Substituting $L = 17 \text{ mH} = 17 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H}$ and $R = 1.7 \text{ }\Omega$:

$\tau = \dfrac{17 \times 10^{-3}}{1.7} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 0.01 \text{ s}$
